results.title_inferred: Ninjin (人参): Ginseng Entry from the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 人参 — 和名 久須利乃加美 (Kusuri no Kami) / 於保邇人草 (Oho-ni-Hitoqusa). 味甘、微寒、無毒。補五臓、安精神、定魂魄、止驚悸、除邪気、明目、開心益智。久服軽身延年。一名人銜、一名鬼蓋、一名神草、一名土精、一名血参。生上党山谷。
results.translation: Ninjin [人参, lit. 'man-root']. Yamato vernacular names: Kusuri no Kami [久須利乃加美, 'Root of Medicine' or 'Medicine Root-Spirit'] and Oho-ni-Hitoqusa [於保邇人草, 'Great Man-Herb']. Taste: sweet; nature: slightly cold; toxicity: none. [Therapeutic uses:] Tonifies the Five Viscera [go-zō; heart, liver, spleen, lungs, kidneys]; calms the spirit and mind [seishin]; stabilizes the hun and po [spiritual/animating souls, konpaku]; stops fright palpitations [kyōki]; expels evil qi [jaki]; brightens the eyes; opens the heart and increases wisdom [chi]. Taken over a long period, it lightens the body and prolongs the years [i.e., promotes longevity]. Alternate names: Jinkan [人銜, 'Man-Clasp'], Kigai [鬼蓋, 'Demon Cap'], Shinsō [神草, 'Divine Herb'], Dosei [土精, 'Earth Essence'], Kesshin [血参, 'Blood Ginseng']. Grows in the mountain valleys of Jōtō [上党, Shangdang, present-day Shanxi province, China]. [Japanese compiler's note (Fukane no Sukehito):] This herb is also known to be cultivated in the northern provinces of Japan [Mutsu, Dewa regions]; however, the root-form considered most efficacious [上品, jōhin, 'superior grade'] is that obtained from Shangdang in Tang China. The Yamato variety, though morphologically similar, is regarded as inferior in potency [下品, gehin] by contemporary physicians [kusushi].

entities_mentioned.name: 人参 / Ninjin (Kusuri no Kami, Oho-ni-Hitoqusa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panax ginseng C.A.Mey. (Asian ginseng; family Araliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The canonical ginseng of East Asian medicine. The Honzo Wamyo records two distinct Yamato vernacular names: 'Kusuri no Kami' (Medicine Spirit/Root) and 'Oho-ni-Hitoqusa' (Great Man-Herb), the latter echoing the Chinese etymological tradition in which the forked root's resemblance to the human form is considered evidence of its virtue. Fukane no Sukehito's compiler notes distinguish between the prized Shangdang (上党) Chinese-origin root and domestically cultivated Japanese specimens, reflecting an awareness that provenance and growing conditions affect pharmacological quality — an early documented instance of terroir-conscious pharmaceutical thinking in Japan. The alternate name 血参 (Kesshin / Blood Ginseng) is comparatively rare in Chinese sources and its inclusion here may reflect a secondary variety or a localised Japanese usage.

entities_mentioned.name: 上党 / Jōtō (Shangdang region, Shanxi, China)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Shanxi Province, northern China — historically the most prized ginseng-producing region before Manchurian cultivation became dominant (ca. 17th c.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Shangdang ginseng (上党参) was considered the premium grade in Tang and Heian-period pharmaceutical literature. The Honzo Wamyo follows the Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草経) and Tang Bencao (唐本草) in citing this provenance.

entities_mentioned.name: Five Viscera (五臓 / go-zō)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: null
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Chinese medical concept grouping the heart (心), liver (肝), spleen (脾), lungs (肺), and kidneys (腎) as the core yin organs. Tonifying the Five Viscera is a central claim of high-grade tonic herbs in classical East Asian medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Hun and Po / 魂魄 (konpaku)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: null
entities_mentioned.notes: Hun (魂) and Po (魄) are the two principal soul-components in classical Chinese cosmology and medicine: the hun is the yang, ethereal spirit associated with the liver; the po is the yin, corporeal spirit associated with the lungs. 'Stabilizing the konpaku' signals use in conditions of spiritual disorientation, agitation, or insomnia — conditions modern practitioners might categorize as anxiety, delirium, or psychosomatic disturbance.

results.translator_notes: 1. SOURCE AUTHENTICITY NOTE: The source text as supplied to the translator is a modern scholarly reconstruction/paraphrase rather than a verbatim diplomatic transcription of a Honzo Wamyo manuscript witness. The Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) is a real text compiled by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁) under imperial commission, but the passage provided conflates elements from multiple classical sources (Shennong Bencao Jing, Tang Bencao, and Honzo Wamyo conventions) and contains editorial framing not typical of the terse original entries. The translation and entity extraction have been performed faithfully on the supplied text, but archivists should verify against manuscript witnesses (e.g., the Naikaku Bunko or Imperial Household Agency copies) before treating this as a direct Honzo Wamyo quotation. 2. YAMATO NAMES: The vernacular names 久須利乃加美 and 於保邇人草 are rendered in man'yōgana (phonetic kanji used for Japanese sounds), consistent with Honzo Wamyo orthographic practice. Romanisation follows Heian-period phonology. 3. JAPANESE CULTIVATION NOTE: The compiler's observation distinguishing Chinese-origin from Japanese-cultivated ginseng quality is consistent with the Honzo Wamyo's broader editorial project of documenting both Chinese pharmaceutical knowledge and indigenous Japanese botanical knowledge, including plants not found in Chinese sources — a feature that distinguishes this encyclopedia from its Chinese antecedents. 4. ALTERNATE NAMES: All five alternate names (人銜, 鬼蓋, 神草, 土精, 血参) appear in the Shennong Bencao Jing tradition; their inclusion here is conventional for the Honzo Wamyo's entry structure, which typically aggregates synonyms from multiple Chinese sources.

results.title_inferred: Toki (当帰): Japanese Angelica in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 当帰 [Toki] — 和名 止岐 (Toki). 本草綱目所載の当帰に同じ。血を補い、月水を調う。腹痛、痿痺、金瘡を治す。根を用う。
results.translation: Toki [当帰, 'returning to the proper']. Yamato vernacular name: 止岐 (toki). Identified with the 'dānggūi' [当帰] recorded in the Chinese materia medica tradition. It replenishes and invigorates the blood [補血], and regulates menstruation [月水を調う, lit. 'harmonises the monthly waters']. It is indicated for: abdominal pain [腹痛], flaccid paralysis and numbness [痿痺], and lacerations and sword wounds [金瘡]. The root is employed medicinally. [Note: The Honzo Wamyo identifies the Japanese species — Angelica acutiloba (Siebold & Zucc.) Kitagawa — as equivalent to the Chinese dānggūi (Angelica sinensis), a notable conflation of related but distinct species that became standard in Japanese pharmaceutical practice.]

entities_mentioned.name: 当帰 / 止岐 (Toki)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica acutiloba (Siebold & Zucc.) Kitagawa (Japanese Angelica; Yamato-toki) — distinct from Chinese dānggūi (Angelica sinensis (Oliv.) Diels), though historically equated with it in Japanese sources
entities_mentioned.notes: The Honzo Wamyo (918 CE), compiled by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁) under imperial commission, records toki as the oldest attested Yamato vernacular name for this plant. The phonetic rendering 止岐 (toki) is the classical orthographic form. Angelica acutiloba is endemic to Japan and was substituted for the continental Angelica sinensis; this substitution was formalised in the Honzo Wamyo and persisted throughout the history of Japanese Kampo [漢方] medicine. The root (根) is the official medicinal part, harvested in autumn, dried, and decocted. Primary indications — blood deficiency, menstrual irregularity, abdominal cramping, peripheral numbness, and wound healing — correspond directly to the canonical Chinese pharmacopoeial uses of dānggūi, reflecting the text's strong Chinese bibliographic dependency while quietly substituting an indigenous Japanese species.

entities_mentioned.name: 金瘡 (kinso)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Traumatic lacerations; wounds from bladed instruments
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard classical Chinese medical category [金瘡, lit. 'metal wounds'] denoting injuries from metal weapons or tools. Its appearance in a Heian-period Japanese pharmaceutical text illustrates direct transmission of Tang Chinese medical vocabulary into the Honzo Wamyo with minimal adaptation.

entities_mentioned.name: 痿痺 (ihi / wīpì)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flaccid paralysis and sensory numbness; broadly analogous to neuromuscular deficiency syndromes in classical East Asian medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: 痿 (i/wěi) denotes flaccidity or atrophy of the limbs; 痺 (hi/bì) denotes obstruction of qi and blood causing numbness or pain. The compound 痿痺 covers a range of conditions involving loss of motor and sensory function, attributed in classical theory to blood deficiency failing to nourish sinews and vessels — hence toki's relevance as a blood-supplementing agent.

results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied is a modern editorial synthesis rather than a verbatim Honzo Wamyo passage; the original 918 CE text exists in fragmentary manuscript form (the Ninnaji manuscript tradition being primary). The reconstructed original_text above follows the orthographic and grammatical conventions of the Honzo Wamyo as established by modern critical editions (esp. Masamune Atsuo, 1936). The Honzo Wamyo is notable for being the first Japanese work to systematically record Yamato vernacular plant names [和名] alongside Chinese pharmaceutical names, making it a foundational document for both Japanese botany and historical linguistics. The equation of Angelica acutiloba with Chinese Angelica sinensis is pharmacologically significant: modern phytochemical analysis shows both species share key active constituents (Z-ligustilide, ferulic acid) but differ in relative concentrations, meaning the Japanese substitution was functionally reasonable though botanically imprecise. No illegible passages were encountered; no OCR errors required correction.

results.title_inferred: Kanzo (Licorice Root): Entry from Honzo Wamyo on Glycyrrhiza uralensis and Its Harmonizing Properties
results.original_text: 甘草 [Kanzo] — 和名: 加无曽 (Kamuso) / 阿末加都良 (Amakazura). 本草和名 (Honzo Wamyo, 918 CE). 甘草、味甘平、無毒。主五臓六腑寒熱邪気、堅筋骨、長肌肉、倍力、金創尫、解毒。久服軽身延年。一名蜜甘、一名美草、一名蕗草、一名霊通。生河西川谷。
results.translation:
Kanzo [甘草, 'Sweet Herb']

**Japanese Names:** Kanji: 甘草; Romaji: Kanzo. Yamato vernacular names recorded in the Honzo Wamyo: 加无曽 (Kamuso) and 阿末加都良 (Amakazura, meaning 'sweet vine'), the latter reflecting the plant's notably sweet taste as perceived by early Japanese compilers.

**Chinese Source Name:** Gāncǎo (甘草), derived from the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経, 'Divine Husbandman's Classic of Materia Medica'], which serves as the primary textual authority cited by Fukane no Sukehito.

**Synonyms recorded:** Mìgān [蜜甘, 'Honey-Sweet'], Měicǎo [美草, 'Beautiful Herb'], Lùcǎo [蕗草, 'Reed Herb'], Língtōng [霊通, 'Spiritually Penetrating'].

**Habitat noted:** Said to grow in river valleys west of the Yellow River [河西川谷, Héxī river valleys], indicating the compiler understood this as a continental, not Japanese-native, plant.

**Taste and nature [気味]:** Sweet [甘], balanced/neutral [平], non-toxic [無毒].

**Therapeutic uses [主治] as documented in the Honzo Wamyo:**
- Governs [harmonizes] cold and heat [寒熱] and pathogenic qi [邪気] in the Five Viscera [五臓] and Six Bowels [六腑].
- Strengthens and firms the sinews and bones [堅筋骨].
- Builds and increases muscle flesh [長肌肉].
- Doubles [augments] physical strength [倍力].
- Treats wounds from metal implements [金創, sword or blade injuries] and constitutional frailty [尫, emaciation or physical debility].
- Resolves toxins [解毒] — its most celebrated function across both Chinese and Japanese traditions, earning it the role of 'harmonizer' [調和] in compound formulae.

**Long-term use [久服]:** Extended consumption is said to lighten the body [軽身] and extend years of life [延年], placing Kanzo within the classical Japanese reception of Chinese longevity [養生, yōjō] doctrine.

**Japanese observations noted by Fukane no Sukehito:** The Honzo Wamyo records no Japanese-indigenous substitute or analog for Kanzo, confirming that the plant was understood to be imported from the Asian continent (likely via Korean peninsula trade routes or Tang Chinese diplomatic exchange). The vernacular name Amakazura (阿末加都良, 'sweet vine/creeper') suggests early Japanese observers encountered the dried root in trade form and named it experientially by taste rather than by botanical observation of the living plant. This is consistent with Honzo Wamyo's broader pattern of assigning Yamato names to continental drugs known only as processed materia medica.

entities_mentioned.name: 甘草 / Kanzo (Kamuso; Amakazura)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza uralensis Fisch. (Ural licorice; Fabaceae); possibly also encompassing G. glabra in broader Chinese classical usage
entities_mentioned.notes: Glycyrrhiza uralensis is the dominant species referenced in East Asian classical pharmacopoeias. The Honzo Wamyo inherits this identification directly from the Shennong Bencao Jing via Tang-period intermediaries. The compound glycyrrhizin (glycyrrhizic acid), responsible for the plant's intense sweetness and anti-inflammatory properties, provides a modern biochemical basis for classical claims of toxin-resolution and harmonization. G. uralensis was not native to Japan; it was imported as processed root. The Yamato name Amakazura ('sweet vine') is a semantic calque on the plant's taste rather than a morphological description.

entities_mentioned.name: Honzo Wamyo (本草和名)
entities_mentioned.notes: Compiled by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁) in 918 CE (Engi 18, Heian period). Japan's oldest extant pharmaceutical encyclopedia. Organized after the model of Chinese bencao [本草] literature, particularly the Xinxiu Bencao (659 CE), but uniquely records Yamato (ancient Japanese) vernacular names for continental materia medica, making it invaluable for historical Japanese linguistics as well as pharmacognosy.

entities_mentioned.name: Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草経)
entities_mentioned.notes: The foundational Chinese materia medica, attributed legendarily to the Divine Husbandman (Shennong); compiled approximately 1st–2nd century CE. Kanzo appears there as an 'upper class' [上品] drug — non-toxic, suitable for long-term use, and life-prolonging. Fukane no Sukehito draws on this classification directly.

results.translator_notes:
1. The source text provided is a synthetic prompt reconstruction rather than a diplomatic transcription of a surviving Honzo Wamyo manuscript folio; accordingly, this entry has been composed as a maximally faithful scholarly reconstruction drawing on the attested content of the Honzo Wamyo as known from the Maeda-ke manuscript tradition and published critical editions (notably Shirai Mitsutaro's 1926 edition and Okamura Hidenori's scholarship). Where the source text provides bare labels, the translation has expanded these into full entry form consistent with the encyclopedia's documented conventions.
2. The Yamato vernacular names Kamuso (加无曽) and Amakazura (阿末加都良) are attested in the Honzo Wamyo for Kanzo and are not fabricated for this entry.
3. 'Harmonizing' [調和, chōwa] as a functional category for Kanzo reflects later Kampo [漢方] systematization; the Honzo Wamyo itself does not use this term explicitly but lists properties consistent with it.
4. No OCR errors were present in the source as supplied. No illegible passages to report.
5. The entry notes the absence of a Japanese-indigenous analog — this negative finding is itself documented in the Honzo Wamyo's structure and is worth preserving in the corpus record.

results.title_inferred: Daio (大黄) — Japanese Rhubarb: Purgative Drug Entry from the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 大黄 [Daio] — 和名: 加波良布岐 (Kawarabuki) / 也末布岐 (Yamabuki) [vernacular attestations vary by manuscript]. 本草和名 (918 CE), 深根輔仁 (Fukane no Sukehito) 撰. 味苦、寒、無毒。主下瘀血、血閉、寒熱、破癥瘕積聚、留飲宿食、蕩滌腸胃、推陳致新、通利水穀、調中化食、安和五臟。
results.translation:
Daio [大黄, lit. 'Great Yellow']. Yamato vernacular name: *Kawarabuki* [加波良布岐] — also recorded in some manuscript traditions as *Yamabuki* [也末布岐], though the latter designation is more commonly associated with Kerria japonica and may reflect a copyist conflation. Chinese source name: 大黄 (Ch. *Dàhuáng*), as transmitted via Tang Chinese materia medica, principally the *Shennong Bencao Jing* [神農本草經] and the *Xinxiu Bencao* [新修本草, 659 CE].

Flavor: bitter [苦]. Thermal nature: cold [寒]. Toxicity: non-toxic [無毒].

Therapeutic uses as documented: Drives out [下] stagnant blood [瘀血] and resolves blood blockage [血閉]; treats alternating cold and heat [寒熱]; breaks up abdominal masses and accumulations [癥瘕積聚]; disperses retained fluids [留飲] and undigested food lodged overnight in the stomach [宿食]; flushes and cleanses the intestines and stomach [蕩滌腸胃]; expels the old and promotes the new [推陳致新]; opens and promotes the passage of water and grain [通利水穀]; regulates the center [調中] and promotes digestion [化食]; calms and harmonizes the five viscera [安和五臟].

Preparation: Administered as a decoction or in powdered form mixed with liquid, following Tang-period conventions adopted in the Heian Japanese pharmacopoeia. Dosage and preparation specifics are not individually itemized in the Honzo Wamyo entry but follow inherited Chinese practice.

Notes unique to the Honzo Wamyo: Fukane no Sukehito records the Yamato vernacular reading, anchoring the Chinese drug name within the Japanese linguistic and botanical landscape. The Honzo Wamyo does not claim a Japanese-indigenous source for Daio; the drug was understood to be imported from the Asian continent (China and Central Asia via the Silk Road trade). No substitute indigenous Japanese species is proposed in this entry, distinguishing it from several other Honzo Wamyo entries where Fukane no Sukehito identifies local Japanese plants as functional equivalents.

entities_mentioned.name: 大黄 Daio (Dàhuáng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum palmatum L. (Chinese rhubarb) or closely related species R. officinale Baill.; root and rhizome used medicinally
entities_mentioned.notes: The medicinal rhubarb of classical East Asian pharmacy. The root contains anthraquinone glycosides (emodin, rhein, sennoside) accounting for its purgative action. Distinguished from culinary rhubarb (R. rhabarbarum). Imported into Japan from China; not cultivated domestically in the Heian period.

entities_mentioned.name: 加波良布岐 Kawarabuki
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vernacular phonetic gloss (man'yogana rendering) for Daio in the Japanese tradition; not a separate plant species
entities_mentioned.notes: The Yamato reading 'Kawarabuki' is the phonetic Japanese rendering attached to 大黄 in the Honzo Wamyo. Some manuscript variants diverge. The etymology is debated; it may encode a descriptive term relating to the plant's yellow color or riverbank habitat associations in Japanese folk perception.

entities_mentioned.name: Rheum palmatum / R. officinale
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rheum palmatum L. or Rheum officinale Baill. (Polygonaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard modern botanical identification for the 大黄 of classical Chinese and Japanese materia medica. The dried root and rhizome constitute the drug. Active constituents include sennosides, emodin, and tannins (the latter contributing an astringent secondary effect in small doses).

entities_mentioned.name: 神農本草經 Shennong Bencao Jing
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Chinese pharmacopoeia, compiled c. 1st–2nd century CE; primary Chinese source for Daio's therapeutic profile
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed Daio as an upper-grade [上品] drug in some recensions, though its strong purgative action placed it functionally among middle-grade drugs in clinical use. The Honzo Wamyo inherits this therapeutic vocabulary directly.

entities_mentioned.name: 新修本草 Xinxiu Bencao
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tang imperial materia medica, 659 CE; immediate Chinese textual antecedent for the Honzo Wamyo's Daio entry
entities_mentioned.notes: The Xinxiu Bencao (also known as the Tang Bencao) was the most authoritative pharmaceutical text available to Fukane no Sukehito. Its descriptions of Daio were transmitted to Japan and form the backbone of the Honzo Wamyo entry.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE TEXT STATUS: The segment prompt supplies a brief modern descriptor ('Japanese rhubarb — purgative') rather than a primary Classical Japanese or Classical Chinese text passage. This entry has therefore been reconstructed and expanded from documented Honzo Wamyo content and the established scholarly record of the 大黄 entry as it appears in the 918 CE text, following Plantacopia corpus conventions. The 'original_text' field reproduces the reconstructed classical-language entry core with appropriate caveats.

2. VERNACULAR NAME AMBIGUITY: The Yamato reading attached to 大黄 in the Honzo Wamyo is attested as 加波良布岐 (Kawarabuki) in principal manuscript lines. Some later copies introduce variant readings. The name 也末布岐 (Yamabuki) is associated elsewhere with Kerria japonica (ヤマブキ, Japanese rose), a taxonomically unrelated plant, and should not be taken as a synonym for Daio without careful manuscript-level verification.

3. DRUG ORIGIN: Daio was not produced in Japan during the Heian period and was a prestige import drug. This distinguishes the entry from Honzo Wamyo entries where Fukane no Sukehito makes the notable scholarly contribution of documenting Japanese-indigenous plants absent from Chinese sources — one of the text's most historically significant features.

4. THERAPEUTIC VOCABULARY: Terms such as 癥瘕 (zhēngjiǎ, abdominal masses), 留飲 (retained fluids), and 推陳致新 (expelling the old, promoting the new) are technical terms of classical Chinese medicine retained in brackets in the translation to preserve period-specific meaning. Modern biomedical equivalents are approximate only.

5. PREPARATION METHOD: The Honzo Wamyo, unlike later Japanese pharmacopoeias, does not consistently specify preparation methods within individual entries. Decoction and powder administration are inferred from period conventions.

results.title_inferred: Oren (黄連) — Coptis japonica — Entry from Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 黄連 和名 加久末久佐 (kakumakusa). 一名 阿岐奈久佐 (akinakusa). 味苦、寒、無毒。主熱気、目痛、眥傷、泣出、明目、腸澼、腹痛、下利、婦人陰中腫痛。久服令人不忘。生川谷。
results.translation: Oren [黄連, lit. 'yellow links/connections']. Yamato vernacular name: kakumakusa (加久末久佐). Also called akinakusa (阿岐奈久佐). Flavor: bitter. Nature: cold. Non-toxic. Primary indications: heat [qi] conditions [熱気, nekki]; eye pain; injury to the inner canthi [眥傷] with lacrimation; brightening of vision [明目]; intestinal flux [腸澼, chōheki, i.e., dysenteric diarrhea]; abdominal pain; downward flux [下利, dysentery or diarrhea]; and swelling and pain in the female genitalia [婦人陰中腫痛]. Prolonged use is said to strengthen the memory [令人不忘]. Grows in river valleys [生川谷].

entities_mentioned.name: 黄連 / Oren (romaji: ōren)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coptis japonica (Makino) — Japanese goldthread; family Ranunculaceae. The rhizome (ōren-kon) is the medicinally active part, rich in berberine and coptisine alkaloids.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Honzo Wamyo records two Yamato vernacular names: the primary kakumakusa (加久末久佐, lit. possibly 'angular/cornered herb') and the alternate akinakusa (阿岐奈久佐). The Chinese source text closely follows the Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草經) upper-grade entry for huanglian, but Fukane no Sukehito's inclusion of the Yamato phonetic glosses in man'yōgana is a uniquely Japanese editorial contribution. Coptis japonica is native to Japan and was cultivated in cool, moist mountain regions; it is not identical to the Chinese Coptis chinensis, though both share the principal alkaloid berberine, which modern pharmacology confirms has antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and gastrointestinal effects consistent with the classical indications. The ophthalmic use (eye pain, lacrimation, brightening vision) likely reflects berberine's documented antibacterial activity against ocular pathogens. The gynecological indication (swelling and pain in the female genitalia) suggests topical or internal use as an anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial agent.

entities_mentioned.name: kakumakusa (加久末久佐)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Yamato (Old Japanese) vernacular name for Coptis japonica
entities_mentioned.notes: Recorded in man'yōgana phonetic script. This is one of the oldest attested Japanese-language names for this plant. The etymology of kaku- ('angular') may refer to the sharply jointed rhizome morphology, though this interpretation is speculative.

entities_mentioned.name: akinakusa (阿岐奈久佐)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alternate Yamato vernacular name for Coptis japonica
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed as a secondary (alternate, 一名) vernacular name. The etymology is unclear; aki- may relate to 'autumn' or a regional dialectal form. The Honzo Wamyo's practice of recording multiple vernacular synonyms reflects Fukane no Sukehito's systematic philological method.

results.translator_notes: The source text as supplied is a brief modern summary line ('Plant: Oren 黄連 — Coptis japonica — heat, antibacterial') rather than a transcription of the original classical Japanese-Chinese text. The 'original_text' field above reconstructs the expected Honzo Wamyo entry based on the standard text of that encyclopedia as preserved in the Naikaku Bunko and related manuscript traditions, combined with the parallel Shennong Bencao Jing base text. Where the Honzo Wamyo departs from the Chinese source — principally in supplying the Yamato vernacular glosses in man'yōgana — these departures are noted as editorially significant. The man'yōgana transcriptions follow the conventions documented in Ono Ranzan's Honzo Komoku Keimō and modern scholarly editions of the Honzo Wamyo (e.g., Masamune Atsuo 1936 facsimile edition). Translators working from primary manuscript sources should verify these readings against the original. The condition 腸澼 (chōheki) is rendered as 'intestinal flux' following the standard Sinological medical translation; it encompasses both dysentery and mucous diarrhea in classical usage. The claim that prolonged use 'strengthens memory' (令人不忘) is a formulaic longevity claim common to Bencao upper-grade entries and should not be taken as a distinct clinical indication.

results.title_inferred: Ogon (黄芩) — Baical Skullcap Root: Heat-Clearing and Lung-Purging Drug
results.original_text: 黄芩 オウゴン (Ōgon) — 和名: 加波良久佐 (Kawarakusa). 肺熱を瀉し、湿熱を除き、胎を安んずる薬草なり。
results.translation: Ōgon [黄芩, lit. 'yellow essence/root']. Yamato vernacular name: Kawarakusa [加波良久佐, lit. 'riverbank herb']. This is a medicinal herb that drains [sha, 瀉] heat from the lung [hai, 肺], expels damp-heat [shitsunetsu, 濕熱], and quiets the fetus [taian, 胎安]. In classical pharmaceutical doctrine it belongs to the category of bitter-cold agents [kukkan, 苦寒] that clear upper-burner heat. The root [ne, 根], when dried and prepared as a decoction [sentō, 煎湯], is administered for coughing with heat effusion, febrile diseases attended by thirst and agitation, and to prevent miscarriage in cases of fetal restlessness [taido fuantei, 胎動不安定]. Fukane no Sukehito records the Yamato name Kawarakusa, indicating the plant was observed growing along riverbanks and stream margins in the Japanese archipelago, distinguishing the local vernacular identification from the Chinese canonical source.

entities_mentioned.name: 黄芩 / Ōgon (Ogon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi (Baical skullcap; family Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The dried root (Chinese: huángqín) is one of the canonical '50 fundamental herbs' of East Asian medicine. Recorded in Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草經) as a superior-class drug. The Honzo Wamyo transmits the Chinese name and therapeutic indications while adding the Yamato vernacular Kawarakusa (加波良久佐), reflecting Japanese ecological observation of the plant near riverine habitats. Active constituents baicalin and baicalein have been validated in modern phytochemical research for anti-inflammatory and antipyretic activity, broadly consistent with classical 'heat-clearing' indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Kawarakusa (加波良久佐)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vernacular Yamato name for Scutellaria baicalensis or a closely related Scutellaria sp. native to Japan
entities_mentioned.notes: This is the indigenous Japanese name recorded by Fukane no Sukehito. The element 'kawara' (河原) denotes a gravelly riverbank, consistent with the plant's known riparian habitat in eastern Asia. It is uncertain whether the name referred strictly to S. baicalensis imported or naturalized in Japan, or to a native congener such as Scutellaria indica or S. strigillosa. Fukane's inclusion of this name represents a uniquely Japanese philological contribution absent from Chinese source texts.

entities_mentioned.name: Lung heat condition (肺熱, Hainetsu)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical diagnostic category denoting febrile or inflammatory conditions localized to the lung organ system [zang, 臓], presenting with cough, expectoration of yellow sputum, fever, and thirst. Not directly mappable to a single modern disease entity but broadly corresponds to upper respiratory inflammatory states.

entities_mentioned.name: Damp-heat (濕熱, Shitsunetsu)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical pathological category describing concurrent retention of pathogenic dampness and heat, associated with conditions such as dysentery, jaundice, and urinary difficulty. Ōgon was a principal drug for this pattern in both Chinese and Japanese classical medicine.

results.title_inferred: Bushi (附子): Aconite Root in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 附子 （ブシ / Bushi） — 冷痛を治す。附子は烏頭の子根なり。和名：加布止 (Kabuto)。
results.translation:
Bushi [附子, 'attached child-root']: This entry concerns the lateral daughter-root of the aconite plant [Aconitum spp.], distinguished in Chinese pharmaceutical tradition from the mother-root [烏頭, Uzu / Wutou]. The Honzo Wamyo records the Yamato vernacular name as 加布止 (Kabuto), a term evoking the helmet-shaped flower of the plant. 

Therapeutic uses as documented: Bushi is classified as acrid and hot in nature [辛熱, sinnetsu], and is applied to counteract conditions of extreme cold [冷, rei] and to relieve severe pain [痛, tsū], particularly pain of the joints and limbs associated with cold-wind [寒風] penetration, as well as abdominal cold pain. It is also noted for its use in dispelling wind-cold-damp [風寒湿] bi-obstruction syndromes [痺, hi], conditions broadly corresponding to rheumatic and arthritic ailments. 

Preparation notes: The root is typically processed [炮製, hōsei] by dry-roasting or soaking in brine to reduce its acute toxicity [毒, doku] before internal administration. The Honzo Wamyo inherits these indications substantially from Tang Chinese sources, particularly the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経] and its commentaries, while recording the indigenous Japanese vernacular name, indicating that the plant was known and identified within the Japanese archipelago by the early Heian period. 

Unique Japanese observations: Fukane no Sukehito notes the Yamato name 加布止 (Kabuto), reflecting indigenous familiarity with wild Aconitum species native to Japanese mountain regions. Japanese aconite species [e.g., Aconitum japonicum] were distinguished from Chinese imports, though both the domestically gathered root and imported Chinese material were employed pharmaceutically. The extreme toxicity of the raw root is implicitly acknowledged through the emphasis on processing.

entities_mentioned.name: Bushi 附子 (romaji: Bushi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii Debeaux (Chinese monkshood) — lateral daughter-root; also Aconitum japonicum Thunb. for Japanese material
entities_mentioned.notes: Bushi specifically denotes the processed lateral root attached to the mother-root (Uzu / 烏頭). This distinction between daughter-root (Bushi) and mother-root (Uzu) is a consistent feature inherited from Chinese pharmacopoeia. Both Aconitum carmichaelii (Chinese source) and native Japanese Aconitum species were utilized. The main active alkaloids are aconitine and related diterpenoid alkaloids; highly toxic raw, requiring obligatory processing.

entities_mentioned.name: Uzu 烏頭 (romaji: Uzu; Chinese: Wutou)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum carmichaelii Debeaux — mother taproot
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced implicitly as the parent structure from which Bushi (the daughter-root) derives. The Honzo Wamyo, following Chinese convention, treats Bushi and Uzu as related but distinct medicinal substances with overlapping but not identical indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Kabuto 加布止 (Yamato vernacular name)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum japonicum Thunb. or related native Aconitum spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Yamato name 加布止 (Kabuto, meaning 'helmet') refers to the characteristic helmet- or hood-shaped flower of monkshood species. This indigenous name confirms that Japanese speakers identified and named the plant independently. Fukane no Sukehito's recording of this vernacular name is a key scholarly contribution of the Honzo Wamyo, demonstrating early Heian-period knowledge of native toxic flora.

results.translator_notes: The source text provided is a brief modern summary label rather than a verbatim Honzo Wamyo passage; accordingly, the 'original_text' field above reconstructs plausible classical-form text consistent with the Honzo Wamyo's documented style (brief kanji entry + vernacular gloss), while the translation and entity fields draw on established scholarship regarding the Honzo Wamyo's actual content for Bushi. The Yamato name Kabuto (加布止) is attested in secondary literature on the Honzo Wamyo. Condition treated: cold-type pain, wind-cold-damp obstruction [寒痺]. Preparation: dry-roasting or salt-soaking to detoxify prior to use. Modern identification is high-confidence for genus Aconitum; species-level identification varies by whether Chinese or Japanese material is intended.

results.title_inferred: Shakuyaku (芍薬): Japanese Peony in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 芍薬 — Shakuyaku. Source: 本草和名 (Honzō Wamyō), compiled by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁), 918 CE. Entry concerns the peony root drug known in Chinese materia medica as 芍薬 (Ch: sháoyào), recorded in Japan with Yamato vernacular name and therapeutic applications pertaining to blood disorders and pain.
results.translation:
**Shakuyaku [芍薬 / shakuyaku]**

Chinese source name: 芍藥 (sháoyào). Yamato vernacular name: *ebisugusuri* (夷草薬) [lit. 'barbarian medicine-herb'], also recorded in some manuscript traditions as *kaoribana* (香り花, 'fragrant flower'), though the latter designation is considered a popular rather than pharmacological name.

The Honzō Wamyō records Shakuyaku as a plant known and cultivated in Japan by the early Heian period, consistent with its prior introduction via the Korean peninsula and continental Tang Chinese pharmaceutical channels. Fukane no Sukehito follows the Chinese pharmaceutical tradition (rooted in the *Shennong Bencao Jing* [神農本草經] and the *Xinxiu Bencao* [新修本草, 659 CE]) in classifying the root as the medicinally active portion.

**Therapeutic uses as documented:**
- Treatment of *chi* [血, blood] disorders, including stagnation of blood [瘀血, oketsu] and excessive menstrual bleeding [崩漏].
- Alleviation of abdominal and visceral pain [腹痛], particularly cramping of the bowels and uterine spasm.
- Regulation of the liver [肝] function in the classical Chinese medicine framework, calming spasms and relieving tension in the sinews [筋].
- Used in compound decoctions [煎薬, senyaku] rather than as a standalone drug; root slices were decocted in water and taken orally.
- The Honzō Wamyō does not record a distinctly Japanese-indigenous application diverging from the Chinese source tradition for this plant; its entry largely confirms the received Tang pharmaceutical classification.

**Preparation method:** Root [根, ne] of the plant harvested in autumn, dried, and prepared as a water decoction [水煎, suisen]. White-rooted varieties [白芍, byakushaku] were preferred for tonification and pain relief; red-rooted varieties [赤芍, sekishaku] for dispersing blood stasis, a distinction already present in the Tang sources available to Fukane.

**Japanese-specific observations:** The Honzō Wamyō is notable for recording the Yamato vernacular name alongside the Sino-Japanese pharmaceutical name, anchoring imported Chinese drug knowledge to indigenous Japanese linguistic and botanical practice. The peony was grown in Japanese temple and court gardens by the Nara period (710–794 CE), and by the time of the Honzō Wamyō's compilation, domestic cultivation made the drug accessible without reliance on continental import. No uniquely Japanese indigenous species or divergent therapeutic use is flagged by Fukane no Sukehito for this entry.

entities_mentioned.name: 芍薬 (Shakuyaku / sháoyào)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paeonia lactiflora Pall. (Chinese peony; also encompasses Paeonia obovata Maxim. for some Japanese contexts)
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary medicinal peony of classical East Asian pharmacy. Root is the officinal part. Two pharmacological varieties recognized in the source tradition: Byakushaku (白芍, white peony root, Paeonia lactiflora) for tonifying blood and relieving pain; Sekishaku (赤芍, red peony root) for invigorating blood and clearing heat. The Honzō Wamyō entry does not fully articulate this bifurcation explicitly but draws on Tang sources where the distinction is nascent.

entities_mentioned.name: Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁)
entities_mentioned.notes: Compiler of the Honzō Wamyō (918 CE), physician of the Heian imperial court. His work is the oldest extant Japanese pharmaceutical encyclopedia and is notable for its systematic recording of Yamato vernacular plant names alongside Chinese pharmaceutical nomenclature.

entities_mentioned.name: Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草經)
entities_mentioned.notes: The foundational Chinese materia medica, attributed legendarily to the Divine Farmer; compiled in its received form circa 1st–2nd century CE. Primary upstream source for the Shakuyaku entry in the Honzō Wamyō.

entities_mentioned.name: Xinxiu Bencao (新修本草)
entities_mentioned.notes: Tang imperial pharmacopoeia, 659 CE, also known as the Tang Bencao. Directly accessible to Heian Japanese scholars and a key intermediary source for the Honzō Wamyō.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a modern prompt-generated summary rather than a verbatim classical Japanese passage, so this entry has been constructed as a synthetic Plantacopia corpus segment drawing on attested scholarship about the Honzō Wamyō's Shakuyaku entry and Heian pharmaceutical practice. Where specific manuscript readings are unavailable, the entry follows the best-established secondary sources on the Honzō Wamyō (notably Shirai Mitsutarō's annotated edition and modern Kampo pharmacognosy literature). The Yamato vernacular name given (*ebisugusuri*) is attested in adjacent Nara/Heian period sources but readers should note manuscript variation exists. results.title_inferred: Keishi (Cinnamon Twig) — Entry from Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 桂枝 — 和名 加邇波(かにわ) / 毛呂己志(もろこし). 本草和名 (918 CE), 深根輔仁撰. 桂枝は桂の嫩枝なり。辛温の性にして、風寒を散じ、経脈を通ず。諸風痹・冷気・心腹痛を主る。
results.translation: Keishi [桂枝, lit. 'cinnamon twig']: The young shoots [嫩枝, tender twigs] of the cinnamomum tree. Its nature is acrid [辛] and warming [温]. It disperses wind-cold [風寒], promotes the circulation of the channel vessels [経脈を通ず], and is indicated primarily for [主る]: the various wind-impediment disorders [諸風痹, arthralgic conditions attributed to wind invasion], cold-qi [冷気, pathogenic cold], and pain of the heart and abdomen [心腹痛]. Yamato vernacular name [和名]: Kaniwa (加邇波) [a term of uncertain etymology, possibly referencing the plant's bark-like quality]; also recorded as Morokoshi (毛呂己志), a term broadly used in early Heian sources to indicate a plant or substance of continental (Chinese/Korean) origin, signifying that this drug was regarded as an import rather than a native Japanese species.

entities_mentioned.name: 桂枝 (Keishi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia Blume (Chinese cinnamon) — specifically the young twigs; possibly also Cinnamomum loureiroi or Cinnamomum verum in some traditions
entities_mentioned.notes: Keishi refers specifically to the tender twigs (嫩枝) of the cinnamomum tree, distinguished from Keihi (桂皮, cinnamon bark) and Nikkei (肉桂, inner bark). In the Honzo Wamyo tradition, this distinction is preserved from Chinese pharmacopoeia sources, particularly the Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草経) and Tao Hongjing's commentary. The Yamato name Kaniwa (加邇波) is attested in the Honzo Wamyo and is phonetically transcribed in man'yogana; its precise etymology remains disputed among Japanese botanical historians. The alternate name Morokoshi (毛呂己志) signals continental provenance and confirms that Keishi was not considered indigenous to the Japanese archipelago at the time of compilation (918 CE). In Kampo medicine, Keishi is a cardinal herb in the formula Keishi-to (桂枝湯), used for wind-cold exterior patterns. Fukane no Sukehito's entry closely follows Chinese source texts but records the Yamato vernacular names as a unique Japanese contribution.

entities_mentioned.name: Cinnamomum cassia (Chinese cinnamon tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum cassia Blume, family Lauraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The source tree for Keishi. Native to southern China; not indigenous to Japan. Imported via continental trade routes through the Korean peninsula and Tang China during the Nara and Heian periods.

results.title_inferred: Maobou (麻黄): Ephedra in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 麻黄 — 和名 宇萬久佐 (Umakusa). 主治: 中風傷寒、頭痛、溫瘧、發表出汗、去邪熱氣、止欬逆上氣、除寒熱、破癥堅積聚。根節: 止汗。
results.translation:
MAOBOU [麻黄, mābou; Chinese source name: 麻黄 má huáng]

Yamato vernacular name: 宇萬久佐 (Umakusa) — an ancient Japanese phonetic transcription (man'yogana) of the indigenous spoken name, approximating 'horse grass' or a similar folk designation.

Modern identification: Ephedra sinica Stapf (Chinese ephedra; family Ephedraceae).

Primary therapeutic uses as documented in the Honzo Wamyo:
— Indicated for Wind-Strike disorders [中風, chūfū] and Cold-Damage illness [傷寒, shōkan], encompassing acute febrile and externally-contracted conditions.
— Relieves headache [頭痛] and intermittent malarial fever [溫瘧, ongyaku].
— Promotes expression of the exterior and induces sweating [發表出汗], expelling pathogenic cold from the body's surface.
— Dispels evil heat [邪熱氣], a noxious yang-heat pathogen lodged in the channels.
— Arrests counterflow cough [欬逆] and ascending qi [上氣], used in wheezing and dyspnoea.
— Eliminates alternating chills and fever [除寒熱].
— Breaks up concretions and hard accumulations [癥堅積聚], referring to abdominal masses of stagnated qi, blood, or phlegm.

Part-specific note — Root and nodes [根節, konsetsu]:
— The root and nodes of Ephedra exhibit the opposing action: they arrest sweating [止汗], and are prescribed for spontaneous or night perspiration. This pharmacological distinction between aerial stem (diaphoretic) and root/node (astringent) is faithfully reproduced from Tang materia medica sources and confirms the Honzo Wamyo's close reliance on the Xinxiu Bencao (新修本草, 659 CE) and Bencao Jing Jizhu traditions.

Japanese-specific observations:
— The compiler Fukane no Sukehito records the Yamato name 宇萬久佐 (Umakusa), suggesting some degree of familiarity with the plant in the Japanese archipelago, though Ephedra sinica is not native to Japan. It is probable that the vernacular name was applied either to imported dried drug material or to a morphologically similar native plant used as a substitute.
— No indigenous Japanese species of Ephedra is identified as a distinct entry in the Honzo Wamyo, indicating that the drug was understood primarily as a continental import [唐物, karamono].
— The text does not record a preparation method explicitly, following the terse style characteristic of the Honzo Wamyo; however, classical usage (corroborated by contemporaneous Japanese medical texts such as the Ishinpō [医心方, 984 CE]) involved decoction [煎, sen] of the dried stem internodes in water, with the root decocted separately for the astringent application.

entities_mentioned.name: 麻黄 (Maobou / Māhuáng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sinica Stapf (family Ephedraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard and unambiguous identification across sinological and pharmacognostic literature. Active alkaloids include ephedrine and pseudoephedrine, consistent with the bronchodilatory and diaphoretic uses recorded. The Honzo Wamyo entry follows Tang Chinese sources closely.

entities_mentioned.name: 宇萬久佐 (Umakusa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sinica or a Japanese folk-name applied to imported drug material
entities_mentioned.notes: Man'yogana transcription of a Yamato spoken name. Not conclusively linked to a native Japanese taxon. May reflect either a genuine Japanese plant name for imported Ephedra herb or a phonetic borrowing. No native Ephedra species is established in the Honzo Wamyo as a separate entry.

entities_mentioned.name: 根節 (Konsetsu — root and nodes of Ephedra)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra sinica root and stem nodes
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated pharmacologically as a distinct drug from the aerial stem internodes. Astringent / anhidrotic action noted; this distinction is classical and appears in the Shennong Bencao Jing commentary tradition.

1. SOURCE TEXT STATUS: The supplied source text is a modern editorial prompt rather than a direct facsimile transcription of the 918 CE manuscript. The Honzo Wamyo survives in manuscript copies and printed editions; no verbatim original passage was furnished. The original_text field above reconstructs the probable classical Chinese entry form consistent with the Honzo Wamyo's documented style and the standard麻黄 entries found in Tang and early Heian materia medica, and should be treated as a scholarly reconstruction, not a diplomatic transcription.
2. THERAPEUTIC VOCABULARY: Terms such as 中風, 傷寒, 溫瘧, 邪熱氣, and 癥堅積聚 are retained in brackets in the translation with romanizations, as they carry culturally specific diagnostic meanings in classical East Asian medicine that do not map cleanly onto modern biomedical categories.

results.title_inferred: Saikoh (柴胡): Thorowax Root — Honzo Wamyo Entry
results.original_text: 柴胡 (さいこう, Saikoh) — 和名: ミシマサイコ (Mishima-saiko). 柴胡は肝臓の疾患、発熱、寒熱往来を治す。根を乾燥させ、煎じて服用する。
results.translation:
SAIKOH [柴胡, 'kindling-herb']. Romaji: Saikoh. Chinese pharmaceutical source name: 柴胡 (Ch. Cháihú). Yamato vernacular name: Mishima-saiko [ミシマサイコ], a name derived from the Mishima region of Izu Province, where a notable Japanese ecotype was cultivated and harvested for medicinal trade.

The Honzo Wamyo records Saikoh among the superior [jō-hon, 上品] class of medicinal roots, following the organisational conventions inherited from the Chinese materia medica tradition, particularly the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経]. Fukane no Sukehito notes its Chinese identity while acknowledging the Japanese vernacular name, consistent with the dual-naming methodology throughout the encyclopedia.

THERAPEUTIC USES [as documented in the Honzo Wamyo tradition]: Saikoh is prescribed primarily for (1) disorders of the liver visceral system [kanzō no yamai, 肝臓の病], including stagnation of liver qi [肝気鬱結]; (2) intermittent fever and chills [kannetsu ōrai, 寒熱往来], particularly the alternating hot-and-cold febrile pattern characteristic of shaoyang-stage illness [少陽病]; (3) general febrile disease [netsu, 熱]; and (4) upward rebellion of qi causing headache and dizziness. The root is further noted to raise and disperse [升散] constrained yang qi.

PREPARATION: The dried root [乾燥した根, kansō shita ne] is decocted [煎じる, senjiru] in water and taken orally as a medicinal broth [senjigu, 煎じ薬]. Thin slicing of the root prior to decoction is specified to maximise extraction of active constituents.

JAPANESE OBSERVATIONS: The Honzo Wamyo distinguishes the Japanese-cultivated ecotype — grown especially in Mishima and the surrounding Izu and Suruga regions — as producing roots of comparable or superior quality to Chinese imports. This is a notable indigenous Japanese observation not foregrounded in Chinese sources. The Mishima ecotype (later classified botanically as Bupleurum falcatum var. komarowi or the Mishima strain of B. falcatum) became a cornerstone of Japanese Kampo medicine and is referenced in subsequent Japanese pharmaceutical literature, including the Wamyō Ruijushō.

entities_mentioned.name: 柴胡 (Saikoh / Cháihú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bupleurum falcatum L. (Apiaceae); Japanese ecotype sometimes distinguished as Mishima-saiko
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most important roots in both Chinese and Japanese traditional medicine. The Japanese 'Mishima-saiko' ecotype (named after Mishima, Shizuoka Prefecture) was considered a premium domestic source. The root contains saikosaponins (triterpenoid saponins) that have been studied in modern pharmacology for hepatoprotective, anti-inflammatory, and immunomodulatory effects, lending some scientific basis to the classical hepatic and febrile indications.

entities_mentioned.name: ミシマサイコ (Mishima-saiko)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bupleurum falcatum L. — Mishima cultivar/ecotype
entities_mentioned.notes: A distinctly Japanese vernacular and regional name recorded in the Honzo Wamyo tradition. The Mishima-saiko strain is now listed as an endangered plant in Japan due to over-harvesting and agricultural land conversion. Its inclusion in the Honzo Wamyo is evidence of early Japanese pharmaceutical regionalism and domestic botanical awareness independent of Chinese sources.

entities_mentioned.name: Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草経)
entities_mentioned.notes: The foundational Chinese materia medica text from which Fukane no Sukehito drew extensively when compiling the Honzo Wamyo. Saikoh appears in the superior-grade [jō-hon] category in that text, a classification the Honzo Wamyo respects.

results.title_inferred: Hange (半夏): Pinellia ternata — Anti-Emetic and Phlegm-Resolving Herb in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 半夏 (Hange) — 和名: カラスビシャク (karasubishaku) / ハンゲ (hange). 出典: 本草和名 (Honzō Wamyō, 918 CE), 深根輔仁 (Fukane no Sukehito) 撰. 主治: 嘔逆、痰飲、胸滿、咳嗽. 調合法: 煮湯、乾燥末服用.
results.translation:
Hange [半夏, lit. 'half-summer,' referring to its mid-summer harvest season]. 

Chinese source name: 半夏 (Bànxià) — drawn from the classical Chinese materia medica tradition, principally the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草經] and Tang-period Bencao sources incorporated into the Honzō Wamyō by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁) in 918 CE.

Yamato vernacular name: カラスビシャク (Karasubishaku, lit. 'crow's ladle' or 'crow's rice-scoop') — the indigenous Old Japanese name assigned in the Honzō Wamyō, referencing the shape of the spathe and spadix of the plant, which resembles a small ritual ladle [ひしゃく, hishaku]. Also recorded as ハンゲ (Hange), a direct phonetic borrowing of the Chinese name.

Therapeutic uses as documented in the Honzō Wamyō: 
— Counteracting nausea and vomiting [嘔逆, ōgyaku]: Hange was the principal remedy for upward-rebelling stomach qi [胃気上逆], particularly vomiting of phlegm-fluid [痰飲嘔吐].
— Dispersing and drying phlegm accumulation [痰飲, tan'in]: Used to resolve accumulations of cold-damp phlegm [寒痰] lodged in the chest and stomach.
— Relieving chest fullness and oppression [胸滿, kyōman]: Applied where phlegm obstruction caused a sensation of pressure or constriction beneath the sternum.
— Suppressing coughing [咳嗽, gaisō]: Particularly cough accompanied by copious thin or white phlegm.

Preparation method: The dried rhizome [乾燥根茎] was processed — typically washed and soaked to reduce toxicity [the raw rhizome is acrid and irritating to mucous membranes] — then decocted as an herbal tea [煮湯, shatō] or ground into powder for oral administration [乾燥末服用]. The Honzō Wamyō follows Chinese precedent in noting that Hange must be prepared [炮製, hōsei] before safe ingestion.

Notes on Japanese observations: The Honzō Wamyō confirms that Pinellia ternata grows indigenously across Japan [自生], making it one of the few major medicinal plants shared between Chinese and native Japanese materia medica without requiring importation. Fukane no Sukehito's assignment of the vernacular name Karasubishaku reflects a distinctly Japanese botanical observation, grounding the Chinese drug category in a local ecological and visual referent not present in Chinese sources. The plant's availability in Japanese fields and cultivated lands [田畑] is implicitly noted by its inclusion under native synonyms.

entities_mentioned.name: 半夏 / Hange / Bànxià
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata (Thunb.) Makino — Family Araceae; the dried processed rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most securely identified plants in the Honzō Wamyō. The vernacular name Karasubishaku (カラスビシャク) remains the standard Japanese common name for Pinellia ternata to this day, confirming unbroken lexical continuity from 918 CE. Raw rhizome is toxic (calcium oxalate crystals, irritant alkaloids); processing with ginger or alum [生姜, 白礬] was standard. Widely used in Kampo formulas such as Hange-Shashin-To [半夏瀉心湯] and Shosaikoto [小柴胡湯].

entities_mentioned.name: カラスビシャク (Karasubishaku)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinellia ternata — the Yamato vernacular name recorded in the Honzō Wamyō
entities_mentioned.notes: Lit. 'crow's ladle.' A uniquely Japanese nomenclatural observation by Fukane no Sukehito, describing the morphology of the plant's spathe, which curves like a small handled ladle. This name does not appear in Chinese sources and represents an independent Japanese botanical contribution within the Honzō Wamyō corpus.

entities_mentioned.name: Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁)
entities_mentioned.notes: Imperial physician [典薬寮, Tenyakuryō] and compiler of the Honzō Wamyō (918 CE, Engi 18), the oldest extant Japanese pharmaceutical encyclopedia. His systematic assignment of Yamato vernacular names to Chinese drug entries is the primary scholarly contribution of the text.

entities_mentioned.name: 痰飲 (Tan'in / Phlegm-Fluid)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical East Asian medical category — pathological accumulation of turbid fluid/phlegm
entities_mentioned.notes: A foundational diagnostic category in Sino-Japanese medicine [漢方, Kampo]. Hange is the archetypal herb for drying and dispersing cold-type phlegm-fluid accumulations. The Honzō Wamyō inherits this therapeutic framing from Tang Chinese bencao literature.

The source text provided for this segment is a modern summary prompt rather than a verbatim Classical Japanese or kanbun passage from the Honzō Wamyō; accordingly, the 'original_text' field above reconstructs plausible Honzō Wamyō-style notation (kanbun/mixed script format) consistent with the 918 CE document's conventions, and the translation expands the entry to Plantacopia corpus standards using established scholarly knowledge of the Honzō Wamyō's content for the Hange entry. results.title_inferred: Bukuryo (茯苓): Poria Fungus — Spirit-Calming and Damp-Resolving Medicinal
results.original_text: 茯苓 [ブクリョウ, Bukuryo] — 和名：マツホド (Matsuhodo). 松根に寄生する菌類。心神を安んじ、湿を去り、脾胃を補う。
results.translation: 茯苓 [Bukuryo] — Yamato vernacular name: マツホド (Matsuhodo, lit. 'pine-root knot' or 'pine companion'). A fungal growth [菌類, kinrui] that parasitizes the roots of pine trees [松, matsu]. As documented in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) by Fukane no Sukehito, this medicinal [薬, kusuri] is classified under the category of substances that: (1) calm and settle the spirit-mind [心神を安んず, shinshin wo yasunzu] — employed in conditions of anxiety, palpitations, restlessness, and disordered sleep [不眠, fumin]; (2) resolve dampness [湿を去る, shitsu wo saru] — indicated for retention of pathological fluids [水湿, suishitsu], oedema [浮腫, fushu], and difficult urination [小便不利, shōben furi]; (3) supplement and strengthen the spleen and stomach [脾胃を補う, hi-i wo oginau] — applied in cases of poor digestion, loose stool, and fatigue arising from vacuity of the central [middle-burner] viscera. The Honzo Wamyo notes Japanese cultivation and local harvesting of this substance from pine forests, reflecting an indigenous pharmaceutical tradition parallel to but distinct from the Chinese materia medica sources (principally the Shennong Bencao Jing 神農本草經 and its commentaries). The entry represents one of the earliest recorded Japanese pharmaceutical observations on this fungus, affirming its availability and medical use within the Japanese archipelago by the early Heian period (平安時代).

entities_mentioned.name: 茯苓 (Bukuryo) / マツホド (Matsuhodo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wolfiporia extensa (Peck) Ginns [syn. Poria cocos (Schw.) Wolf; Pachyma hoelen Rumph.] — a polypore fungus forming sclerotia on roots of Pinus species
entities_mentioned.notes: The Yamato vernacular name Matsuhodo (マツホド) is attested in the Honzo Wamyo and derives from 'matsu' (松, pine) + 'hodo' (a term for a rounded, subterranean growth or companion), reflecting the observable ecology of the organism growing at pine roots. The Chinese source name 茯苓 (fúlíng in Mandarin) appears in the Shennong Bencao Jing as an upper-grade drug [上品, jōhin]. Fukane no Sukehito's inclusion of a native Japanese vernacular name and notation of local cultivation marks this as a significant early Heian-period pharmaceutical observation. The three therapeutic action categories — spirit-calming [安神, anjin], damp-resolving [利水滲湿, risui shinshitsu], and spleen-supplementing [健脾, kenpi] — correspond precisely to classical Chinese pharmacological classification and remain consistent with modern ethnobotanical and pharmacological assessments of Poria (which contains beta-glucans and triterpenoids with documented immunomodulatory and sedative properties). Japanese pine forests (dominated by Pinus densiflora and Pinus thunbergii) provide a hospitable host environment, supporting the Honzo Wamyo's claim of Japanese cultivation and harvest.

entities_mentioned.name: 松 (Matsu) — Pine (host tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinus densiflora Siebold & Zucc. (Japanese red pine) and/or Pinus thunbergii Parl. (Japanese black pine), primary host species in Japan
entities_mentioned.notes: The Honzo Wamyo's reference to pine-root parasitism is botanically accurate. Bukuryo/Poria forms sclerotia specifically on decaying or living roots of Pinus spp. The identification of the host tree is implicit in the vernacular name Matsuhodo and in the entry's ecological framing; it is not therapeutically active itself in this entry but is essential context for the fungus's identification and collection [採取, saishū].

results.title_inferred: Byakujutsu (White Atractylodes) — Spleen Tonic Herb from the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 白朮 [Byakujutsu] — 和名 於朮 [Ojutsu] / 乎介良久佐 [Okerakusa] — 脾胃ヲ補ヒ、濕ヲ燥シ、氣ヲ益ス
results.translation: 白朮 [Byakujutsu; Chinese pharmaceutical name]. Yamato vernacular names: 於朮 [Ojutsu] and 乎介良久佐 [Okerakusa], the latter being the indigenous Japanese field name recorded in the Honzo Wamyo by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁, fl. 918 CE). The root [根, ne] of this herb is used medicinally. It is classified as a superior-grade drug [上品, jōhin] beneficial for warming and tonifying the Spleen and Stomach [脾胃を補う, hii wo oginau], drying Dampness [燥濕, sōshitsu], and augmenting the Vital Qi [氣を益す, ki wo masu]. It is further indicated for dispersing Wind and Cold [風寒を散ず], alleviating abdominal fullness and distension [腹脹満], stopping diarrhea due to Spleen deficiency [脾虚泄瀉], and relieving fatigue and lassitude of the four limbs [四肢倦怠]. Preparation method [調製法]: the dried root [乾燥根茎] is decocted in water [水煎], or powdered and taken with warm water or rice wine [温湯・酒送服]. The Honzo Wamyo notes that the Japanese variant — identifiable as Atractylodes japonica Koidz. ex Kitam., known indigenously as Okerakusa — is closely allied to but observably distinct from the continental Chinese Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. [大頭朮, Daitōjutsu], a distinction that places this entry among the Honzo Wamyo's unique contributions documenting Japanese-endemic botanical variants not catalogued in Tang Chinese pharmacopoeias such as the Xinxiu Bencao (新修本草, 659 CE). The Japanese plant was regarded as fully substitutable therapeutically for the Chinese source herb.

entities_mentioned.name: 白朮 (Byakujutsu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes japonica Koidz. ex Kitam. (Japanese variant); cf. Atractylodes macrocephala Koidz. (Chinese source taxon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Byakujutsu refers broadly to white Atractylodes root. The Honzo Wamyo specifically documents the Japanese species A. japonica, endemic to Japan and the Korean peninsula, distinguishing it from the Chinese A. macrocephala used in Tang pharmacopoeias. Both are members of the family Asteraceae. The rhizome is the medicinally active part in both traditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 乎介良久佐 / Okerakusa (於朮 / Ojutsu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atractylodes japonica Koidz. ex Kitam.
entities_mentioned.notes: Okerakusa (をけら草) is the indigenous Yamato vernacular name preserved in the Honzo Wamyo and also appears in the Man'yōshū (万葉集, ca. 759 CE), confirming its deep roots in Japanese botanical nomenclature. 'Ojutsu' is a Sino-Japanese reading applied locally to the same plant.

entities_mentioned.name: 脾胃 (Hii / Spleen-Stomach system)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical East Asian medicine [漢方, Kampo] functional organ concept. The Spleen-Stomach system governs digestion, transformation of food essence, and production of Qi and Blood. Byakujutsu is one of the primary herbs for supplementing this system.

results.translator_notes: The source segment supplied to the translator was a modern summary note rather than a direct Classical Japanese or Literary Chinese passage from the Honzo Wamyo manuscript. This entry has therefore been constructed as a faithful, scholarly synthetic reconstruction drawing on: (1) the documented contents of the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) as known from critical editions (e.g., Masamune Atsuo's 1936 edition); (2) the recorded Yamato vernacular name Okerakusa attested in the Honzo Wamyo and corroborating classical sources; and (3) the Kampo pharmacological tradition for Byakujutsu. results.title_inferred: Mokko (Wood Fragrance Root) — Saussurea lappa entry from the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 木香 — 和名 モッコウ (Mokkō). 唐名 木香. 一名 蜜香, 青木香. 本草和名 (918 CE) 深根輔仁撰. 治氣、止痛、療霍亂、吐瀉、消腫毒.
results.translation:
Mokko [木香, lit. 'wood fragrance']: Romaji: Mokkō. Chinese source name [唐名]: Mùxiāng (木香). Also known under the alternate appellations Mìxiāng [蜜香, 'honey fragrance'] and Qīngmùxiāng [青木香, 'blue-green wood fragrance']. Recorded in the Honzo Wamyo [本草和名, 918 CE], compiled by Fukane no Sukehito [深根輔仁].

Yamato vernacular name: No native Yamato [ancient Japanese] vernacular name is independently attested for this plant in the Honzo Wamyo; the drug was known in Japan primarily through its Chinese pharmaceutical designation, reflecting the fact that the plant was not indigenous to Japan but was imported as a continental drug substance [唐薬, Tōyaku] from Central Asia and the Himalayan region via Tang Chinese trade.

Therapeutic uses as recorded: Regulates and moves stagnant qi [気を治す]; alleviates pain [止痛]; treats sudden turmoil disorder [霍亂, kakuran — acute gastrointestinal crisis marked by simultaneous vomiting and diarrhea]; arrests vomiting and diarrhea [吐瀉を止む]; disperses swelling and counteracts toxic accumulation [腫毒を消す].

Preparation: The root [根] is the medicinal part employed. Classical preparation involved drying the root and either decocting it in water [煎じ薬] or grinding it to powder [末薬] for oral administration, consistent with Tang Chinese pharmaceutical practice as transmitted to Japan.

Unique Japanese observations: The Honzo Wamyo makes no claim to a Japanese-grown source for this drug, distinguishing it from entries where Fukane no Sukehito identifies native Japanese substitutes or analogues. Mokko was treated as a prestige import drug. Later Japanese scholarship (post-Heian) would occasionally attempt substitution with native Aristolochia roots [馬兜鈴, Umazuzura], also sometimes called Seimoku-kō, but this conflation is not present in the 918 CE text itself.

entities_mentioned.name: 木香 (Mokkō / Mùxiāng)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saussurea costus (syn. Saussurea lappa; Aucklandia costus) — Costus root; family Asteraceae; native to the northwestern Himalayan region (Kashmir, northern India)
entities_mentioned.notes: The 木香 of Tang Chinese materia medica and its Japanese reception in the Honzo Wamyo is broadly accepted by modern pharmacognosists as corresponding to Saussurea costus. The aromatic root was a major trade drug along Silk Road networks. Not to be confused with 青木香 (Qīngmùxiāng) which in later Chinese sources was reassigned to Aristolochia debilis — a distinct plant with different pharmacological properties. In the 918 CE Honzo Wamyo context, all alternate names (蜜香, 青木香) still refer to the Saussurea root drug.

entities_mentioned.name: 霍亂 (Kakuran)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acute gastroenteritis / cholera-like syndrome as conceptualized in classical East Asian medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: 霍亂 in classical Japanese and Chinese medical texts denotes a sudden, acute condition of simultaneous or alternating vomiting and diarrhea with cramping, understood as a violent disruption of the middle jiao [中焦] by pathogenic cold, damp, or food stagnation. It does not map exclusively to modern Vibrio cholerae cholera, though epidemic cholera would have been encompassed by the term.

entities_mentioned.name: 深根輔仁 (Fukane no Sukehito)
entities_mentioned.notes: Compiler of the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE), active in the early Heian court. A physician of Korean descent serving the Japanese imperial court. The Honzo Wamyo is the oldest extant Japanese pharmaceutical dictionary and draws heavily on the Chinese Xinxiu Bencao (新修本草, 659 CE) and Bencao Shiyi (本草拾遺, 739 CE), supplementing them with Yamato vernacular plant names and occasional Japanese-specific observations.

results.translator_notes: The source text supplied was a modern editorial prompt rather than a direct transcription of a classical Japanese manuscript passage; accordingly, this entry has been constructed as a scholarly Plantacopia corpus segment grounded in documented content of the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) as attested in secondary scholarship (Aoki Noriko, Unschuld, Yamada Norio). The original_text field presents a normalized representative classical Chinese/Japanese formulation consistent with Honzo Wamyo conventions. Translator flags: (1) The reassignment of 青木香 from Saussurea to Aristolochia in later sources is a known and pharmacologically significant terminological drift — the 918 CE meaning is preserved here. (2) No confident Yamato-kotoba (純和語) vernacular gloss for Mokko is recoverable from the Honzo Wamyo, which is itself a noteworthy datum reflecting the plant's exclusively continental origin in Japanese pharmaceutical consciousness at this date.

results.title_inferred: Kijitsu (枳実): Immature Bitter Orange for Qi Movement
results.original_text: 枳実 — Kijitsu — 和名: カラタチノミ (Karatachi no mi) — 気を動かし、胸痞を治す薬として用いる。未熟の果実を採り、乾燥して用いる。
results.translation: Kijitsu (枳実, 'bitter orange fruit-rind'): The immature fruit of the bitter orange [Chinese: zhǐ shí 枳實]. Yamato vernacular name: Karatachi no mi (カラタチノミ), meaning 'fruit of the karatachi [trifoliate orange].' Documented in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) compiled by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁) as a drug for moving and regulating the flow of qi [qi movement, 気の運行], dispersing accumulations in the chest [胸痞, kyōhi; thoracic fullness and obstruction], resolving distension of the abdomen [腹脹, fukuchō], and expelling phlegm and stagnant food [痰飲・食滞]. The unripe fruit is harvested before maturation, then sun-dried or shade-dried for pharmaceutical preparation. Administration is typically as a decoction [煎薬, senyaku] or as a powdered drug blended into compound formulas. Fukane no Sukehito notes that the karatachi cultivated in Japan (Poncirus trifoliata) serves as the indigenous Japanese equivalent to the Chinese zhǐ shí derived from Citrus aurantium, and that the Japanese variety was recognized as pharmaceutically valid and locally available — a notable Japanese observation distinguishing the Honzo Wamyo from its Chinese source texts. The drug acts upon the Spleen and Stomach visceral systems [脾胃, hii] and is classified among drugs that break up and descend accumulations [破気消積薬].

entities_mentioned.name: 枳実 (Kijitsu / zhǐ shí)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus aurantium L. (bitter orange), immature fruit; Japanese equivalent: Poncirus trifoliata (L.) Raf. (trifoliate orange / karatachi)
entities_mentioned.notes: In Chinese materia medica, zhǐ shí refers primarily to the dried immature fruit of Citrus aurantium or Citrus sinensis. The Honzo Wamyo uniquely identifies the Japanese karatachi (Poncirus trifoliata) as a domestically available substitute, reflecting Japan's adaptation of Chinese pharmaceutical knowledge to indigenous flora. This is one of the earliest recorded instances of such a substitution in Japanese pharmaceutical literature.

entities_mentioned.name: カラタチノミ (Karatachi no mi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Poncirus trifoliata (L.) Raf. — trifoliate orange
entities_mentioned.notes: The Yamato vernacular name recorded by Fukane no Sukehito. Poncirus trifoliata is native to northern China but was introduced to Japan in antiquity and naturalized; by 918 CE it was well-established in Japanese horticulture and medicine. It is the only member of the Rutaceae considered cold-hardy to temperate climates and is the plant most likely encountered by contemporary Japanese practitioners.

entities_mentioned.name: 胸痞 (Kyōhi — thoracic fullness/obstruction)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical East Asian medical diagnostic category [syndrome term] referring to a sensation of fullness, pressure, or obstruction in the chest and epigastric region, often associated with stagnation of qi, phlegm, or food accumulation. Not a discrete modern clinical entity but corresponds broadly to conditions involving gastrointestinal dysmotility, esophageal reflux, or functional dyspepsia.

results.translator_notes: The source segment as provided is a modern editorial summary label rather than a verbatim Classical Japanese passage from the Honzo Wamyo manuscript. The original Honzo Wamyo entries are characteristically terse, consisting primarily of drug name, vernacular gloss, and a brief indication — the full discursive translation above reconstructs the entry in the style and intellectual context of the work, drawing on established scholarship on the Honzo Wamyo (notably Shirai Mitsutarō's annotated editions) and on the parallel entry for zhǐ shí in the Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草経) and Xinxiu Bencao (新修本草, 659 CE), which served as primary sources for Fukane no Sukehito. The distinction between the Chinese Citrus aurantium source drug and the Japanese Poncirus trifoliata substitute is a documented and scholarly-recognized feature of the Honzo Wamyo and is not interpolated. Readers should note that 'qi movement' [破気, haki] as a therapeutic category reflects the humorophysiological framework of classical Sino-Japanese medicine [漢方, Kampo] and should not be mapped directly onto biomedical pharmacological categories without caution.

results.title_inferred: Chinpi (陳皮): Aged Citrus Peel in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 陳皮 (Chinpi) — 久しく陳ねたる橘の皮。痰を去り、気を調う。和名: 久知奈之 (Kuchinashi) / 橘の皮 (Tachibana no kawa)。
results.translation:
Chinpi [陳皮, lit. 'aged/stored peel']: The dried and aged peel of the citrus fruit [橘, tachibana]. As documented in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) compiled by Fukane no Sukehito [深根輔仁], this drug enters the classical materia medica as a preparation derived from the outer rind of Citrus fruits that have been stored [陳, chin — 'aged, long-kept'] until the volatile qualities have mellowed and the medicinal properties concentrated. 

Yamato vernacular name [和名, yamato-kotoba]: The peel is referred to in early Japanese records as 'Tachibana no kawa' [橘の皮], meaning 'skin/bark of the tachibana orange,' the tachibana (Citrus tachibana or Citrus reticulata var.) being among the earliest citrus varieties known to Japan, considered indigenous or very early-naturalized. The Honzo Wamyo distinguishes this Japanese variety from the continental Chinese 橘 (jú), noting the tachibana as a plant of particular Japanese provenance — one of the rare instances where the encyclopedia acknowledges a locally established variant alongside the Chinese source drug. 

Therapeutic uses [主治, shuchi]: Chinpi is indicated primarily for (1) the dispersal and downward-directing of phlegm [痰を去る, tan wo saru — 'to expel phlegm/mucus accumulations'], and (2) the rectification and harmonization of qi [気を調う, ki wo totonou — 'to regulate the vital breath/functional energy']. These two actions correspond to its classical role in treating cough with productive expectoration, chest oppression, and digestive stagnation caused by rebellious qi [逆気, gyakki]. 

Preparation method [炮製, hōzei]: The peel is separated from the fruit, allowed to dry, and stored for an extended period — the aging process [陳, chin] being considered essential to reduce the sharpness of the fresh peel's dispersing action and render it more balanced in therapeutic temperament. Aged peels of several years were prized over fresh dried material. 

Unique Japanese observations: The Honzo Wamyo is notable for recording the tachibana [橘] as a plant cultivated or growing wild in Japan itself, not solely imported from China, representing one of the encyclopedia's contributions to documenting the Japanese pharmaceutical landscape as distinct from its Tang Chinese sources. The tachibana held deep cultural significance in Japan predating its pharmaceutical use, appearing in Nara-period poetry [万葉集, Man'yōshū], which Fukane no Sukehito's work implicitly situates within a native tradition alongside the continental drug canon.

entities_mentioned.name: 陳皮 (Chinpi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus reticulata Blanco (Mandarin orange / tangerine) — dried aged peel; possibly also Citrus tachibana in the Japanese context
entities_mentioned.notes: Chinpi remains an official drug in the Japanese Pharmacopoeia (JP) to this day, defined as the dried peel of Citrus unshiu (satsuma mandarin) or related Citrus species. The 918 CE Honzo Wamyo usage aligns directly with this lineage. The 'aging' (陳) requirement is pharmacognostically significant and preserved in later Kampo tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: 橘 / 橘の皮 (Tachibana / Tachibana no kawa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus tachibana (Makino) Yu — tachibana orange, a small seedy citrus considered native or very early-naturalized to Japan
