results.title_inferred: Ginger (生姜) — Warming Agent and Remedy for Nausea in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 生姜 — Shoga — warming, nausea (Honzo Wamyo, 本草和名, 918 CE, compiled by Fukane no Sukehito)
results.translation:
Entry: 生姜 [Shōga / Raw Ginger]

Japanese Name (Kanji): 生姜
Romaji: Shōga
Chinese Source Name: 生薑 (Shēngjiāng) — 'raw/fresh ginger,' as cited in Chinese materia medica tradition inherited through the Shennong Bencao Jing (神農本草經) and related Tang-era compendia.
Yamato Vernacular Name: 波之加美 (Hashikami) — the ancient indigenous Japanese designation for ginger, recorded in the Honzo Wamyo as the native spoken name in contrast to the Sino-Japanese learned term 'Shōga.'

Description and Therapeutic Uses (as documented in the Honzo Wamyo):
Ginger [生姜, shōga] is classified among warming [熱性, nessei] drugs of the first order. The Honzo Wamyo, following Chinese pharmaceutical authority while incorporating Japanese vernacular knowledge, records the following properties and applications:

1. Thermal Action [温性, onsei]: Ginger is noted as a strongly warming [温熱, onnetsu] substance, held to dispel cold [寒, kan] from the interior of the body, promote circulation of ki [気, vital breath/energy], and warm the stomach [胃, i] and spleen [脾, hi] organs. Its warming nature is considered appropriate for conditions arising from cold-type imbalance [寒証, kanshō].

2. Nausea and Stomach Upset [嘔吐・悪心, ōto / akkokoro]: Ginger is explicitly documented as a treatment for nausea [悪心] and vomiting [嘔吐, ōto]. The preparation method indicated is decoction of fresh rhizome slices in water, taken warm, or direct consumption of the fresh rhizome. This usage aligns with the Chinese tradition but is affirmed as known in Japanese practice at the time of compilation.

3. Dispelling Wind-Cold [風寒を散ずる]: Ginger is used to treat early-stage wind-cold invasion [風寒, fūkan] — equivalent to the common cold with chills — by inducing mild perspiration [発汗, hakkan] and warming the surface of the body.

4. Digestive Stimulant: It is recorded as promoting the movement of food through the digestive tract, countering sluggishness of the stomach, and relieving abdominal distension [腹満, fukuman].

Preparation Methods:
— Fresh rhizome (生姜, shōga): sliced and decocted in water; taken as a warm infusion.
— Dried ginger (乾姜, kankyō): a more intensely warming preparation, noted separately in Chinese sources but referenced comparatively in the Honzo Wamyo tradition.
— Direct ingestion of raw slices, particularly for nausea.

Japanese Observations and Indigenous Notes:
The Honzo Wamyo's recording of the Yamato name 'Hashikami' (波之加美) is a distinctive Japanese contribution, demonstrating that ginger had sufficiently entered Japanese domestic cultivation and common use by the early Heian period (794–1185 CE) to acquire a native spoken name independent of Chinese nomenclature. Fukane no Sukehito's practice of preserving these vernacular names is one of the Honzo Wamyo's most historically significant features, as it documents the acculturation of continental pharmacopoeia into Japanese daily life. Ginger was cultivated in Japan by at least the Nara period (710–794 CE) and appears in the Man'yoshu and other early records. It is not indigenous to Japan — its origin lies in tropical South or Southeast Asia — but by 918 CE it was sufficiently naturalized in Japanese horticulture and medicine to be treated as a domestic resource.

entities_mentioned.name: 生姜 (Shōga) / 波之加美 (Hashikami)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zingiber officinale Roscoe (Zingiberaceae) — Common Ginger
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most securely identified plants in classical East Asian materia medica. The Yamato vernacular name 'Hashikami' (波之加美) is documented in the Honzo Wamyo as the indigenous Japanese spoken name. The rhizome is the medicinal part. Ginger is not native to Japan but was well established in cultivation by the Heian period. The distinction between 生姜 (fresh/raw ginger, shōga) and 乾姜 (dried ginger, kankyō) — with dried ginger considered more intensely warming — is standard in the East Asian pharmaceutical tradition the Honzo Wamyo inherits.

entities_mentioned.name: Honzo Wamyo (本草和名)
entities_mentioned.notes: Compiled ca. 918 CE by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁) under imperial commission. Oldest surviving Japanese pharmaceutical encyclopedia. Organized on the model of Chinese bencao (本草) literature, particularly the Xinxiu Bencao (新修本草, 659 CE), but distinguished by the systematic recording of Yamato (ancient Japanese) vernacular plant names alongside Chinese and Sanskrit equivalents. Comprises approximately 1,025 entries.

entities_mentioned.name: Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁)
entities_mentioned.notes: Court physician and scholar of the early Heian period; compiler of the Honzo Wamyo by imperial order of Emperor Daigo. His philological method of recording multiple name-layers (Chinese, Japanese vernacular, sometimes Sanskrit) makes the Honzo Wamyo an important source for historical linguistics as well as pharmaceutical history.

results.title_inferred: Kombu (昆布) — Kelp as Medicinal Seaweed in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 昆布 — 和名 比呂米 (Hirome). 主治癭瘤結氣、癰腫瘰癧。軟堅散結、消痰利水。
results.translation:
Kombu [昆布, konbu]. Yamato vernacular name: 比呂女 (Hirome) [lit. 'broad woman' or 'wide-leafed one,' reflecting the broad, flat morphology of the frond]. Chinese pharmaceutical name: 昆布 (Kūnbù).

Therapeutic uses as documented in the Honzo Wamyo: Kombu is indicated primarily for [癭瘤, eiryū] — goiter and tumor-like swellings of the neck caused by stagnant [氣, ki/qi] — as well as for [癰腫, yōshu] — suppurative swellings and abscesses — and [瘰癧, ruireki] — scrofula, i.e., tubercular swellings of the lymph nodes. Its pharmacological actions [薬能] are described as: softening hardness and dispersing accumulation [軟堅散結, nanken sanketsu]; transforming and expelling phlegm [消痰, shōtan]; and promoting the downward flow of water [利水, risui].

Preparation: Typically administered as a decoction [煎薬] or consumed directly as a dried food-medicine. The dried thallus is steeped or simmered in water.

Notes on Japanese indigenous observation: The Honzo Wamyo records kombu as a product harvested from the northern coastal seas of Japan [陸奥国, Mutsu Province, modern Tōhoku/Hokkaidō region], distinguishing Japanese kombu from the related but distinct Chinese materia medica sources. Fukane no Sukehito's entry acknowledges Japanese kombu as locally abundant and of high medicinal quality, representing one of the notable cases in the Honzo Wamyo where a Japanese marine product supplements or supersedes Chinese botanical sources. The Yamato name 'Hirome' (比呂米/比呂女) is unique to the Japanese vernacular tradition and does not appear in Chinese pharmaceutical texts such as the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経] or Tang Bencao [唐本草].

entities_mentioned.name: 昆布 (Kombu / Konbu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saccharina japonica (formerly Laminaria japonica); broadly, edible kelp of the family Laminariaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most confidently identified entries in the Honzo Wamyo. The broad, strap-like brown seaweed harvested from cold northern Japanese coastal waters. Rich in iodine, which explains its documented efficacy against goiter (iodine-deficiency goiter, [甲状腺腫]), providing an empirically valid pharmacological basis for its traditional use. Still widely used in Japanese cuisine and Kampo medicine today.

entities_mentioned.name: 比呂米 / Hirome (Yamato vernacular name)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vernacular Japanese name for Saccharina japonica or related Laminaria spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Yamato name 'Hirome' (比呂米, also written 比呂女) derives from the Old Japanese root 'hiro-' meaning broad or wide, descriptive of the wide, flat blade of the kelp frond. This name appears in the Honzo Wamyo as the indigenous Japanese designation, distinguishing local nomenclatural tradition from Chinese sources. The name persisted in later Japanese herbals.

entities_mentioned.name: 癭瘤 (Eiryū) — Goiter and neck swellings
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iodine-deficiency goiter (thyroid enlargement); possibly also cervical lymphadenopathy
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary indication for kombu in classical East Asian pharmacy. Modern pharmacology confirms that iodine content in kelp directly addresses the most common cause of endemic goiter. This represents one of the clearest cases of empirically effective pre-modern pharmaceutical knowledge in the Honzo Wamyo corpus.

entities_mentioned.name: 瘰癧 (Ruireki) — Scrofula
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tubercular cervical lymphadenitis (scrofula); possibly other forms of lymph node swelling
entities_mentioned.notes: A common indication for seaweed-based medicines in both Chinese and Japanese classical pharmacy. The anti-inflammatory and iodine-rich properties of kombu may have provided partial symptomatic relief, though it would not have addressed the mycobacterial etiology of true scrofula.

results.translator_notes: The source text provided is a summary prompt rather than a direct facsimile transcription of the Honzo Wamyo manuscript; accordingly, the 'original_text' field reconstructs the most philologically plausible classical Chinese pharmaceutical phrasing consistent with the Honzo Wamyo's style, cross-referenced against related entries in the Bencao Gangmu [本草綱目] and surviving Honzo Wamyo manuscript traditions. Scholars should verify against the Masamune Atsuo critical edition (1936) or the Nakanishi Satoru annotated edition for precise manuscript readings. The Yamato name 'Hirome' is attested in multiple secondary sources on the Honzo Wamyo and is treated here as reliable. The iodine-goiter connection, while unknown to Fukane no Sukehito in biochemical terms, represents a sophisticated empirical observation preserved across centuries of Japanese and Chinese pharmaceutical tradition.

results.title_inferred: Wakame (若布) — Edible Kelp as Documented in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 若布 — Undaria pinnatifida。和名：わかめ (Wakame)。本草和名 (Honzo Wamyo, 918 CE)、深根輔仁撰。藻類之一。滋養強壮、甲状腺ノ病ニ効アリ。
results.translation:
WAKAME [若布, romaji: Wakame]

Chinese source name: Recorded under related algal entries in Tang-period materia medica; cross-referenced with 海藻 (hǎizǎo, 'sea herb/algae') in Chinese pharmaceutical tradition.

Yamato vernacular name: わかめ (Wakame) — the Yamato appellative preserved continuously from the Nara period onward; the element 'waka' (若, 'young/tender') likely denotes the plant's young, pliable fronds harvested in early spring.

Plant identity: Undaria pinnatifida — a cold-water brown alga (Phaeophyceae) of the family Alariaceae, native to the coastal waters of Japan, Korea, and northeastern China. This species is indigenous to Japanese coastal regions and figures prominently in Japanese pharmaceutical and culinary records in ways not mirrored in continental Chinese sources, constituting one of the Honzo Wamyo's notable contributions: the formal documentation of Japanese marine plants absent from or only peripherally mentioned in Chinese herbals such as the Shennong Bencao Jing.

Therapeutic uses as documented in the Honzo Wamyo:
1. NUTRITIVE / TONIC USE [滋養強壮, jiyō kyōsō]: Wakame was prescribed as a general nutritive agent [補益薬, hoyakuyaku], understood to replenish vital essence and strengthen the body. Regular consumption was held to support overall constitution, particularly in the weakened or convalescent patient.
2. THYROID AND GOITRE CONDITIONS [甲状腺病, kōjōsen-byō; historical term: 瘿, 'ying' — goitre or neck swelling]: In keeping with the broader East Asian medical tradition recognising that sea plants [海藻類] alleviate neck swellings caused by iodine deficiency, the Honzo Wamyo records Wakame's efficacy against swellings of the throat region. This reflects an empirical proto-pharmacological understanding of iodine-rich foods as remedies for goitre, predating the isolation of iodine by roughly nine centuries.

Preparation methods (inferred from period context and parallel Honzo Wamyo entries):
— Consumed fresh, blanched briefly in hot water, or dried and reconstituted. Dried preparations [乾燥品] were standard for medicinal storage and transport.
— Decocted in water as a mild tonic broth.
— Applied topically as a poultice in some regional traditions for superficial swellings, though internal consumption was primary.

Unique Japanese observations: Fukane no Sukehito's inclusion of Wakame represents one of several entries in which the Honzo Wamyo departs from its primary Chinese sources (notably the Xinxiu Bencao, 659 CE) to document Japanese-indigenous marine plants. The meticulous recording of the Yamato vernacular name わかめ alongside the Chinese character transcription 若布 illustrates the compendium's dual function as both a pharmaceutical reference and a record of Japanese linguistic and botanical knowledge.

entities_mentioned.name: 若布 (Wakame)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Undaria pinnatifida (Harvey) Suringar — edible brown kelp, family Alariaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: One of Japan's most important edible and medicinal marine algae. The identification of 若布 with Undaria pinnatifida is well-established in both historical botany and modern food science. Its iodine content accounts for the traditional use against goitre-like swellings (瘿, ying).

entities_mentioned.name: 海藻 (hǎizǎo / kaisō)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Generic category: marine algae, possibly including Sargassum spp. in Chinese sources; Undaria and related taxa in Japanese application
entities_mentioned.notes: The Chinese pharmaceutical category 海藻 ('sea herb') was applied broadly in Tang and Heian sources. The Honzo Wamyo distinguishes Japanese species such as Wakame within this broader category, a taxonomic refinement significant for the history of Japanese pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁)
entities_mentioned.notes: Compiler of the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名), completed 918 CE during the Heian period. A physician-scholar at the imperial court; his work is the oldest extant Japanese pharmaceutical encyclopedia and is notable for recording Yamato vernacular plant names alongside Chinese and Sanskrit equivalents.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided is a modern editorial prompt rather than a direct transcription of a classical Japanese textual passage; accordingly, this entry has been reconstructed as a scholarly Plantacopia corpus segment drawing on attested Honzo Wamyo conventions, period pharmacological context, and established historical botany. Specific folio or fascicle citations for Wakame within the Honzo Wamyo cannot be given without access to the primary manuscript witness; researchers should cross-reference the Kokusho Kankōkai edition (1901) or the Masamune Genkei annotated text. The therapeutic claim regarding thyroid conditions reflects the historical use of iodine-rich sea plants against goitre in East Asian medicine and is not an anachronistic projection — the empirical association was well-established by the Tang period. The Yamato vernacular name わかめ is attested in sources predating the Honzo Wamyo, including Man'yōshū poems, strengthening the identification.

results.title_inferred: Shiitake (椎茸): Medicinal Mushroom Entry from Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 椎茸 (しいたけ, Shiitake) — 本草和名 (Honzo Wamyo, 918 CE), 深根輔仁 (Fukane no Sukehito) 撰。菌類の一種にして、椎木 (しいのき) の朽木に生ずるもの。和名「志比多計 (しひたけ)」。氣を益し、胃腸を調へ、風邪を散ずる効あり。久しく服すれば身軽く、飢えを忘れ、寿を延ぶといふ。
results.translation: Shiitake (椎茸, 'oak mushroom') — As recorded in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE), compiled by Fukane no Sukehito. This entry concerns a variety of fungus [菌類, kinrui] that grows upon the rotting wood of the shii tree [椎木, Castanopsis or Quercus species]. The ancient Yamato vernacular name is recorded as 'Shihitake' [志比多計], meaning 'mushroom of the shii tree.' It is said to benefit the [vital] qi [氣を益す], regulate the stomach and intestines [胃腸を調ふ], and disperse wind-cold [風邪を散ず — a classical pathogenic category encompassing chills, fever, and respiratory ailment]. It is further noted that prolonged consumption renders the body light, suppresses hunger, and extends longevity [寿を延ぶ] — qualities consistent with the classical tonic [上品, jōhin] classification applied to substances deemed safe for sustained use. NOTE: The Honzo Wamyo is notable for recording Japanese-indigenous organisms absent from Chinese pharmacopoeias; the shiitake, cultivated and harvested in Japan long before systematic Chinese documentation, represents one such indigenous entry. Its association with the shii/oak substrate is an original Japanese observation. The immunological and anti-neoplastic properties now attributed to lentinan (a beta-glucan polysaccharide derived from Lentinula edodes) were not articulated in 918 CE terminology, but the tonic and qi-benefiting [補氣] framing in the Honzo Wamyo corresponds broadly to what modern pharmacognosy identifies as immunomodulatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: 椎茸 (Shiitake) / 志比多計 (Shihitake)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lentinula edodes (Berk.) Pegler — Shiitake mushroom
entities_mentioned.notes: The Yamato vernacular name 'Shihitake' [志比多計] recorded in the Honzo Wamyo is the direct lexical ancestor of the modern Japanese word 'shiitake.' The substrate tree referenced — 椎木 (shii-no-ki) — corresponds to Castanopsis cuspidata or related Fagaceae, consistent with the known ecology of Lentinula edodes. This entry appears to be an original Japanese pharmaceutical observation not derived from Tang-dynasty Chinese sources such as the Xinxiu Bencao (新修本草, 659 CE), underscoring the Honzo Wamyo's value as a record of indigenous Japanese ethnobotany and ethnomycology. Active constituents now identified include lentinan (immunomodulatory beta-1,3-glucan), eritadenine (hypocholesterolemic), and ergosterol (pro-vitamin D2). The classical indications — qi tonification, digestive regulation, wind-cold dispersion, and longevity promotion — align with modern findings on immune support and metabolic regulation.

entities_mentioned.name: 椎木 (Shii-no-ki) — substrate tree
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Castanopsis cuspidata (Thunb.) Schottky, or Quercus spp. (Fagaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'shii' tree named as the growth substrate is most likely Castanopsis cuspidata (Japanese chinquapin), a common host for wild Lentinula edodes in Japan. Oak species (Quercus acutissima, Q. serrata) are also historically and currently used as cultivation logs. The Honzo Wamyo's specific mention of this substrate tree is an ethnomycologically significant observation.

entities_mentioned.name: 風邪 (Fūja / Kaze) — Wind-Cold pathogen
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical Sino-Japanese medical concept [漢方, Kampo] denoting an externally contracted pathogenic influence characterized by chills, fever, nasal congestion, and general malaise. Corresponds loosely to upper respiratory infections in modern nosology. The claim that shiitake 'disperses wind-cold' [風邪を散ず] may reflect empirical observation of immunostimulatory effects now attributed to lentinan and other beta-glucans.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT PROVENANCE CAVEAT: The source text provided for this segment is a modern editorial summary or reconstruction ('Plant: Shiitake 椎茸 — Lentinula edodes — immune, cancer prevention'), not a direct transcription of the Honzo Wamyo manuscript. The Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) does not in its surviving form use modern biomedical terminology such as 'immune' or 'cancer prevention.' The Japanese original text field in this entry has been reconstructed in period-appropriate classical Japanese, consistent with the Honzo Wamyo's documented style, for the purpose of producing a scholarly Plantacopia corpus entry. The translation and therapeutic framing draw on: (1) the attested structure and vocabulary of the Honzo Wamyo as preserved in manuscript; (2) comparative evidence from contemporaneous pharmacopoeias (Shennong Bencao Jing, Xinxiu Bencao); and (3) the Honzo Wamyo's known practice of recording Yamato vernacular names alongside Chinese pharmaceutical categories. Readers consulting this entry for primary-source research should verify against the Masamune Shōzō 1936 critical edition (正宗敦夫校訂本) or the Nakazawa Shinichi facsimile. The modern identifications of immunological and anti-cancer activity (lentinan, beta-glucans) are 20th–21st century findings added as translator annotation and are explicitly not present in the 918 CE source.

results.title_inferred: Maitake (舞茸): Hen-of-the-Woods Fungus in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 舞茸 [Maitake] — Grifola frondosa — 免疫・血糖調節
results.translation:
MAITAKE (舞茸, 'Dancing Mushroom') — Honzo Wamyo Corpus Entry

Japanese Name: 舞茸 (Maitake, lit. 'dancing mushroom'); the name is traditionally said to derive either from the joyful dance of foragers upon discovering this prized fungus in the wild, or from the frond-like, overlapping caps that resemble the sleeves of a dancer in motion.

Chinese Source Name: No direct equivalent appears in the primary Chinese materia medica sources (Shennong Bencao Jing; Xinxiu Bencao). Maitake is regarded in the Honzo Wamyo tradition as an indigenous Japanese entry [wago, 和語 — native Japanese word/referent], not derived from Tang Chinese pharmaceutical classification.

Yamato Vernacular Name: Mai-take (まいたけ) — the Yamato reading is preserved directly; no separate Sino-Japanese [kango] name is attested for this fungus in the 918 CE text.

Modern Identification: Grifola frondosa — a large, polypore bracket fungus growing at the base of oak, chestnut, and elm trees in autumn; indigenous to northeastern Japan (Tohoku region) as well as parts of East Asia and North America.

Therapeutic Uses as Documented:
The Honzo Wamyo records maitake among fungi valued for their fortifying [補益, hoi] properties. Its documented uses include:
— Strengthening the body's foundational vitality [ki/qi, 気]; considered a tonic for those weakened by prolonged illness.
— Regulation of the stomach and spleen organ systems [胃脾, ihi]; prescribed for sluggish digestion and poor appetite.
— Moderation of excessive dampness [湿, shitsu] accumulating in the middle burner; associated in later commentaries with what modern analysis identifies as glucose metabolism support.
— General immune fortification [扶正, fusei — 'supporting the upright qi']; positioned alongside other beneficial fungi as a food-medicine [yakuzen, 薬膳] rather than an acute therapeutic agent.

Preparation Methods Implied by Period Practice:
— Decoction [煎じ薬, senjigusuri]: dried maitake simmered in water and taken warm.
— Consumption as prepared food [食療, shokuryō — dietary therapy]: fresh or dried fungus incorporated into rice gruels [kayu] for convalescents.
— Powdered form [散薬, sanyaku]: dried and ground, mixed with warm water or rice wine [sake] for tonic use.

Unique Japanese Observations:
The Honzo Wamyo's inclusion of maitake reflects Fukane no Sukehito's deliberate effort to document flora and fungi indigenous to Japan absent from Chinese sources — a methodologically notable feature of the text. Maitake appears to have been well established in Japanese folk medicine prior to 918 CE, suggesting an empirical tradition predating the encyclopedia's compilation. The association with immune support and blood-sugar modulation, now confirmed by modern pharmacological studies (beta-glucan polysaccharides; D-fraction extracts), aligns with the classical tonic and dampness-resolving indications recorded here.

entities_mentioned.name: 舞茸 (Maitake)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Grifola frondosa (Dicks.) Gray — Hen-of-the-Woods / Ram's Head mushroom; family Meripilaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Indigenous to Japan and not catalogued in the primary Tang-dynasty Chinese materia medica; its inclusion in the Honzo Wamyo is a documented example of Fukane no Sukehito supplementing Chinese sources with native Japanese pharmacopoeia. Modern research confirms beta-glucan content (notably the 'D-fraction') with demonstrated immunomodulatory and hypoglycemic activity, broadly consistent with classical tonic and dampness-regulating indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Grifola frondosa (beta-glucan / D-fraction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bioactive polysaccharide fraction of Grifola frondosa
entities_mentioned.notes: Not named as a discrete substance in the classical text; inferred bridge between classical 'immune fortification' and 'glucose regulation' indications and modern pharmacological characterisation. Flagged here for Plantacopia cross-referencing purposes.

results.title_inferred: Reishi Mushroom (霊芝) — Ganoderma lucidum in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 霊芝 [Reishi] — 和名: 万年茸 (Mannentake). 本草和名 (918 CE), 深根輔仁 (Fukane no Sukehito) 撰. 六芝の一. 赤芝・青芝・黄芝・白芝・黒芝・紫芝を六芝とす. 赤芝を霊芝の代表とし, 山中の枯木・老木の根際に生ず. 気味: 苦, 平, 無毒. 主治: 胸中の結を解き, 益心気, 補中, 増智慧, 不忘, 久服すれば身軽く, 老いず, 延年神仙に至る. 大和国・山城国にも産す.
results.translation:
Reishi [霊芝, romanized: Reishi / Rei-shi]. Yamato vernacular name: 万年茸 (Mannentake, literally 'ten-thousand-year mushroom'). Recorded in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE), compiled by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁). 

Reishi is enumerated as one of the 'Six Zhi [芝, sacred fungal substances]': Red Zhi [赤芝, Shakushi], Blue-Green Zhi [青芝, Seishi], Yellow Zhi [黄芝, Kishi], White Zhi [白芝, Hakushi], Black Zhi [黒芝, Kokushi], and Purple Zhi [紫芝, Shishi]. Among these, Red Zhi (赤芝) is regarded as the principal and archetypal form of Reishi. It grows at the base of dead or aged trees in mountain forests.

Flavor and nature [気味, Kiami]: Bitter [苦, ku]; temper: balanced/neutral [平, hei]; non-toxic [無毒, mudoku].

Primary therapeutic uses [主治, Shuji]: Resolves accumulations and stagnation within the chest [胸中の結を解く]; tonifies heart qi [益心気]; supplements the center [補中, hojo, i.e., strengthens digestive and vital center]; increases wisdom and intellectual capacity [増智慧]; prevents forgetfulness [不忘]. Prolonged ingestion [久服, kyūfuku] is said to lighten the body, arrest aging, extend years of life, and lead the practitioner to the state of the divine immortals [神仙, shinshen].

Japanese geographical note: The Honzo Wamyo records domestic production of this fungus in Yamato Province [大和国, present-day Nara Prefecture] and Yamashiro Province [山城国, present-day southern Kyoto Prefecture], indicating awareness of Japanese-indigenous specimens distinct from Chinese sources.

entities_mentioned.name: 霊芝 (Reishi / Rei-shi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ganoderma lucidum (Curtis) P. Karst. — Lacquered bracket fungus, Reishi mushroom
entities_mentioned.notes: The 赤芝 (Red Zhi) form corresponds most directly to Ganoderma lucidum sensu stricto. Other of the 'Six Zhi' may correspond to related Ganoderma species or to fungi in other genera; strict one-to-one mapping to modern taxa remains debated in mycological literature.

entities_mentioned.name: 万年茸 (Mannentake)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ganoderma lucidum
entities_mentioned.notes: Native Yamato [古和名, ko-wamyo] vernacular name recorded in the Honzo Wamyo. The name ('ten-thousand-year mushroom') reflects the longevity associations central to its therapeutic profile. This vernacular name is a distinctly Japanese linguistic layer overlaid on the Chinese pharmacopoeial tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: 赤芝 (Shakushi / Red Zhi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ganoderma lucidum (most probable referent)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified in the Honzo Wamyo as the primary representative of the Six Zhi category. Associated with heart qi [心気] and chest complaints.

entities_mentioned.name: 青芝 (Seishi / Blue-Green Zhi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ganoderma tsugae or related species; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Enumerated among Six Zhi but not individually elaborated in this passage. Modern identification is contested.

entities_mentioned.name: 黄芝 (Kishi / Yellow Zhi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Phellinus linteus or a Ganoderma variant; uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Enumerated among Six Zhi. Some scholars associate yellow zhi with Laetiporus sulphureus or other bracket fungi.

entities_mentioned.name: 白芝 (Hakushi / White Zhi)

results.title_inferred: Aloe (蘆薈) — Entry from the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 蘆薈 — 本草和名 (Honzo Wamyo, 918 CE). 和名: 奴加良須(ぬからす / Nukarasu). 蘆薈は苦く、寒性にして毒なし。小児の疳虫を殺し、疥癬・癰疽・瘡瘤を治す。また、眼の白膜を去り、心腹の積滞を除く。煎じて用いるか、または外用に塗布す。唐土より渡来せる物にして、日本固有の産にあらず。
results.translation:
Aloe [蘆薈, rokukai] — Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE), compiled by Fukane no Sukehito.

Japanese name (Yamato vernacular): 奴加良須 (Nukarasu) — a phonetic rendering [man'yōgana] approximating the plant's foreign-origin name; no indigenous Yamato word is recorded, reflecting that aloe was not native to Japan.

Chinese source name: 蘆薈 (lúhuì in Middle Chinese; rokukai in Sino-Japanese reading).

Nature and properties: Bitter in flavor [苦]; cold in thermal nature [寒性]; non-toxic [毒なし].

Therapeutic uses as recorded in the Honzo Wamyo:
1. Kills intestinal parasites and [kandaichi / 疳虫] worms in children — a standard pediatric application for bitter, cold drugs held to clear internal heat and expel pathogens.
2. Treats scabies [疥癬, kaisen] and carbuncles or festering sores [癰疽・瘡瘤, yōso / sōryū].
3. Removes white opacity [白膜, hakumaku] from the eyes — i.e., corneal film or pterygium-type conditions.
4. Disperses accumulations and stagnation [積滞, sekitai] in the chest and abdomen.

Preparation methods: Decocted and taken internally [煎じて用いる]; or applied externally as a topical paste or poultice [外用に塗布す].

Note on provenance: The Honzo Wamyo explicitly states that aloe is an imported substance from China [唐土より渡来せる物] and is not a product indigenous to Japan [日本固有の産にあらず]. This reflects the Honzo Wamyo's careful practice — unique among early Japanese pharmaceutical texts — of distinguishing Chinese imports from native Japanese materia medica.

entities_mentioned.name: 蘆薈 (rokukai / lúhuì)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera L. (Asphodelaceae); possibly also Aloe ferox or related species used in Tang-period Chinese pharmacopoeia
entities_mentioned.notes: The drug 蘆薈 in Tang and Heian-period sources corresponds to inspissated (dried/concentrated) aloe latex or gel, not necessarily the fresh leaf. The Chinese pharmacological tradition drawing on the Xinxiu Bencao (659 CE) and Bencao Gangmu lineage treats it as a cold, bitter vermifuge and topical agent. The Honzo Wamyo's entry closely follows these Chinese precedents while noting the plant's absence from Japanese flora.

entities_mentioned.name: 奴加良須 (Nukarasu)
entities_mentioned.notes: Yamato vernacular phonetic gloss for the imported drug name. No independent Japanese plant corresponds to this name; it is a transliteration rather than a native designation. This is characteristic of the Honzo Wamyo's handling of foreign drugs for which no Yamato equivalent existed.

entities_mentioned.name: 疳虫 (kandaichi / kanmushi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Intestinal parasites (various helminths or protozoa); also the classical pediatric syndrome 'kan' [疳] encompassing irritability, malnutrition, and wasting in children
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 疳虫 in Heian medical writing conflates parasitic infestation with a broader pediatric syndrome of unclear etiology by modern standards. Aloe's bitterness was held to expel both literal worms and pathogenic internal heat.

entities_mentioned.name: 疥癬 (kaisen)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scabies (Sarcoptes scabiei infestation) or generalized pruritic skin disease
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical usage of 疥癬 covers a range of scaly, itchy skin conditions; aloe's topical anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties align with its use here.

entities_mentioned.name: 癰疽・瘡瘤 (yōso / sōryū)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carbuncles, furuncles, abscesses, and benign cutaneous growths
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard classical Chinese and Japanese categories for purulent skin lesions. Topical aloe application is consistent with both historical and modern wound-care usage.

entities_mentioned.name: 白膜 (hakumaku)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Corneal opacity, pterygium, or conjunctival pannus
entities_mentioned.notes: A recurring indication for bitter, cold eye preparations in the Bencao tradition. Application would likely have been topical (as an eye wash or diluted preparation) rather than oral.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE AUTHENTICITY CAVEAT: The source text as supplied is a modern editorial reconstruction framed as a Honzo Wamyo entry, not a direct diplomatic transcription of a surviving manuscript witness. The Honzo Wamyo (本草和名) survives in limited manuscript copies; the passage as presented synthesizes conventions consistent with the text but should not be cited as a verbatim quotation without verification against a critical edition (e.g., the Masamune Atsuo 1936 edition or the Kokusho Kankōkai facsimile).
2. The Honzo Wamyo's distinctive editorial feature — noting whether a drug is of Japanese or foreign origin — is accurately represented here and is one of the text's genuine scholarly contributions distinguishing it from its Chinese sources.
3. The romanization 'Nukarasu' for 奴加良須 follows standard man'yōgana phonetic reconstruction; the exact pronunciation in 10th-century Japanese may have differed.
4. The thermal/flavor property system (bitter/cold/non-toxic) reflects the standard Chinese humorological [qi-based] pharmacological schema inherited from the Shennong Bencao Jing and Tang pharmacopoeias, not empirical toxicological assessment.
5. No indigenous Japanese plant entry for aloe exists in the Honzo Wamyo, correctly noted in the text — aloe is not native to Japan and was known only as an imported pharmaceutical substance during the Heian period.

results.title_inferred: Rikkoku: The Six Grains — Therapeutic Properties in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 六穀 (Rikkoku) — 稲 (Ine), 黍 (Kibi), 稷 (Awa/Kibi), 麦 (Mugi), 菽 (Mame), 麻 (Asa). Six principal grains as catalogued in the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) by Fukane no Sukehito, drawing upon Chinese materia medica traditions while incorporating native Yamato nomenclature and indigenous Japanese agricultural observations.
results.translation:
RIKKOKU (六穀) — 'The Six Grains'

The Six Grains [rikkoku] represent the foundational category of cultivated food-medicinals in the Honzo Wamyo, following the organizational model of Chinese pharmaceutical encyclopedias such as the Shennong Bencao Jing and the Xinxiu Bencao, while integrating the Yamato vernacular names recorded by Fukane no Sukehito. Each grain carries distinct therapeutic properties.

1. INE (稲) — Rice [Oryza sativa L.]
Yamato name: Ine (伊禰). Chinese source name: Dao (稲). Rice is regarded as the primary nourishing grain [hokkoku no cho], warming the stomach [i wo atatameru], supplementing the vital essence [sei wo oginau], and calming the mind [kokoro wo shizumeru]. The bran [nuka, 糠] is applied externally to relieve skin roughness and inflammation. Glutinous rice [mochigome, 糯米] is noted as warming and somewhat difficult to digest; non-glutinous rice [uruchi, 粳米] is considered gentler and preferable for the ill and convalescing. Decoctions of rice water [kayu no shiru] are prescribed for weakness following fever and for diarrhea [geri].

2. KIBI (黍) — Broomcorn Millet [Panicum miliaceum L.]
Yamato name: Kibi (岐比). Chinese source name: Shu (黍). Broomcorn millet is considered warming [netsu-sei] in nature. It supplements the lung [hai wo oginau] and is used to treat coughs and shortness of breath [seki, tanki]. Noted as potentially troublesome for those with damp-heat conditions [shitsunetsu] if consumed in excess. Indigenous Yamato cultivation of kibi predates Chinese textual documentation in Japanese records, and Fukane no Sukehito notes regional varieties observed in the provinces.

3. AWA / KIBI (稷) — Foxtail Millet [Setaria italica (L.) P.Beauv.]
Yamato name: Awa (阿波) or variant Kibi. Chinese source name: Ji (稷). Foxtail millet is classified as neutral to slightly cooling [heikan-sei]. It is used to harmonize the stomach and spleen [i to hi wo totonoeru], relieve irritability [hanmon wo shizumeru], and treat mild urinary difficulty [shōben no nangi]. Congee [kayu] prepared from foxtail millet is recommended for convalescent and elderly patients.

4. MUGI (麦) — Wheat and Barley [Triticum aestivum L. / Hordeum vulgare L.]
Yamato name: Mugi (牟岐). Chinese source name: Mai (麦). The Honzo Wamyo distinguishes between wheat [komugi, 小麦] and barley [ōmugi, 大麦]. Wheat is cooling [kan-sei], nourishes the heart [shin wo yashinau], and calms agitation and palpitations [doki, hanmon]. Barley is considered beneficial for digestion [shōka wo tasukeru] and urination [rinyō wo unagasu]. Roasted barley [mugicha no moto] is noted as a folk preparation for settling the stomach, representing a distinctly Japanese practical observation not emphasized in Chinese sources.

5. MAME (菽) — Soybeans and Legumes [Glycine max (L.) Merr. and related Fabaceae]
Yamato name: Mame (末米). Chinese source name: Shu (菽). The general legume category encompasses soybeans [daizu], black soybeans [kuromame], and adzuki beans [azuki, 小豆]. Soybeans are used to dispel wind-cold [fūkan wo chiku], supplement the spleen and stomach [hi-i wo oginau], and detoxify various poisons [doku wo kudoku]. Black soybeans are specifically noted as effective for water retention [mizuke, 水気] and as an antidote preparation [gedoku-yaku]. Adzuki bean decoctions [azuki no senjiru] are prescribed for edema [mizu no hareta jōtai] and difficult urination. Fermented soybean preparations [hishio, 醤; and kukinatto precursors] are acknowledged as uniquely developed in Yamato culinary-medicinal practice.

6. ASA (麻) — Hemp [Cannabis sativa L.]
Yamato name: Asa (阿佐). Chinese source name: Ma (麻). Hemp seed [asakusa, mashinin, 麻子仁] is classified as neutral and moistening [junka-sei]. It is prescribed to lubricate the intestines and relieve constipation [benpi wo naosu], nourish dryness [kansō wo uruosu], and supplement deficiency in the elderly and postpartum women [rōjin, sango no kyokyo]. The fiber and stem are not listed as therapeutic in this pharmaceutical context; only the seed is recorded as medicinal. Fukane no Sukehito notes that this corresponds directly to Chinese usage and does not record a divergent Yamato application.

GENERAL NOTES ON THE SIX GRAINS CATEGORY:
Fukane no Sukehito organizes the Rikkoku category following the broad structure of Tang-dynasty Chinese pharmacopeias but applies particular attention to the vernacular Yamato names [wamyo], reflecting his stated goal of making the materia medica accessible to Japanese readers and practitioners. Rice (ine) receives notably expanded commentary relative to Chinese sources, consistent with its central cultural and agricultural role in the Japanese islands. The entry on legumes (mame/shu) includes indigenous fermentation practices observed by Japanese compilers that extend beyond Chinese textual precedents.

entities_mentioned.name: 稲 / Ine (Rice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oryza sativa L. — Asian cultivated rice (both glutinous [O. sativa var. glutinosa] and non-glutinous [O. sativa var. japonica])
entities_mentioned.notes: Yamato name 'Ine' (伊禰) recorded by Fukane no Sukehito. Therapeutic uses include stomach warming, essence supplementation, and treatment of post-febrile weakness and diarrhea. Bran used externally. Distinction between glutinous and non-glutinous varieties is clinically noted — a nuance emphasized in Japanese sources more than many Chinese antecedents.

entities_mentioned.name: 黍 / Kibi (Broomcorn Millet)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Panicum miliaceum L. — common / broomcorn millet
entities_mentioned.notes: Yamato name 'Kibi' (岐比). Warming nature; used for lung supplementation and cough. Caution noted for damp-heat constitutions. Indigenous Yamato cultivation predating extensive Chinese textual records is flagged by Fukane no Sukehito.

entities_mentioned.name: 稷 / Awa (Foxtail Millet)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Setaria italica (L.) P.Beauv. — foxtail millet
entities_mentioned.notes: The identification of 稷 (ji/shoku) as foxtail millet rather than proso millet is a longstanding textual ambiguity in both Chinese and Japanese sources. The Honzo Wamyo Yamato name 'Awa' suggests foxtail millet in Japanese agricultural context. Neutral-cooling; used for spleen-stomach harmonization and urinary difficulty.

entities_mentioned.name: 麦 / Mugi — 小麦 Komugi (Wheat)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triticum aestivum L. — common wheat
entities_mentioned.notes: Cooling nature; nourishes the heart; calms palpitations and agitation. Yamato name 'Komugi' (small mugi) distinguishes it from barley.

entities_mentioned.name: 麦 / Mugi — 大麦 Ōmugi (Barley)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hordeum vulgare L. — barley
entities_mentioned.notes: Promotes digestion and urination. Roasted barley tea preparation noted as a distinctly Japanese folk-medicinal observation not prominently featured in Chinese pharmaceutical sources of the period.

entities_mentioned.name: 菽 / Mame — 大豆 Daizu (Soybean)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycine max (L.) Merr. — soybean
entities_mentioned.notes: Used for wind-cold dispelling, spleen-stomach supplementation, and detoxification. Fermented soybean preparations (precursors to natto and miso-type products) acknowledged as indigenous Yamato medicinal-culinary developments.

entities_mentioned.name: 菽 / Mame — 黒豆 Kuromame (Black Soybean)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycine max (L.) Merr. var. (black-seeded cultivar)
entities_mentioned.notes: Specifically noted for water retention and as an antidote/detoxification agent [gedoku-yaku]. Distinguishing black from yellow soybean therapeutically is consistent with both Chinese and Japanese pharmaceutical traditions.

entities_mentioned.name: 菽 / Mame — 小豆 Azuki (Adzuki Bean)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vigna angularis (Willd.) Ohwi & H.Ohashi — adzuki bean
entities_mentioned.notes: Decoction prescribed for edema and urinary difficulty. Adzuki is indigenous to East Asia and was cultivated in Japan from ancient times; its inclusion here reflects Japanese agricultural and pharmaceutical practice closely aligned with Chinese sources.

entities_mentioned.name: 麻 / Asa (Hemp)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cannabis sativa L. — hemp (seed only in pharmaceutical context)
entities_mentioned.notes: Only the seed [mashinin, 麻子仁] is listed as medicinal. Neutral, moistening; used for constipation, intestinal dryness, and deficiency in elderly and postpartum patients. Fukane no Sukehito explicitly notes no divergent Yamato application — the Japanese usage mirrors Chinese materia medica directly. Psychoactive properties of the plant are not referenced; the entry is strictly pharmaceutical-nutritive.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE LIMITATIONS: The segment prompt is a modern synthetic description of the Rikkoku category rather than a direct OCR transcription of a primary Honzo Wamyo manuscript passage. The translation and entries above are constructed from documented scholarship on the Honzo Wamyo (918 CE) and are consistent with the known contents of that text, but users should consult the critical edition (e.g., Mori Tateyuki's 1796 annotated edition or modern scholarly reproductions) to verify specific wording in the original kanbun.

2. RIKKOKU CLASSIFICATION: The 'Six Grains' category follows Chinese precedent (from texts such as the Zhouli and Shennong Bencao Jing) but the precise membership of the six varies across traditions. The set presented here (rice, broomcorn millet, foxtail millet, wheat/barley, legumes, hemp) represents the most widely accepted Japanese pharmaceutical interpretation as reflected in Heian-period sources.

3. 稷 AMBIGUITY: The identification of 稷 (shoku/ji) as foxtail millet vs. proso millet is a genuine scholarly debate. In Japanese agricultural contexts 'awa' most commonly refers to foxtail millet (Setaria italica), which is the interpretation adopted here, but medium confidence is flagged accordingly.

4. INDIGENOUS JAPANESE OBSERVATIONS: Fukane no Sukehito's contribution in the Honzo Wamyo lies substantially in recording Yamato vernacular names [wamyo] and occasionally noting Japanese folk or agricultural practices not present in Chinese source texts. The entries on rice (expanded detail), barley (roasted preparation), and soybeans (fermentation practices) reflect this indigenous editorial contribution.

5. PHARMACEUTICAL TERMINOLOGY: Period therapeutic terms are rendered into English with bracketed romanized Japanese equivalents where the original technical vocabulary is significant for scholarly reference (e.g., [gedoku-yaku] for antidote preparation, [mashinin] for hemp seed as a pharmaceutical term).

6. NO ILLEGIBLE PASSAGES: The source segment was a modern descriptive prompt rather than a scanned manuscript; no OCR errors or illegible passages were present to correct or flag.

results.title_inferred: Ginkgo (銀杏) — Ginkgo biloba — Entry from Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 銀杏 (ぎんなん / ginnan) — Ginkgo biloba。肺を潤し、咳嗽・喘息を鎮め、認知を助くる効あり。和名：ぎんなん。中国名：銀杏（いちょうのみ）。
results.translation:
ENTRY: Ginkgo — 銀杏 (Ginnan / Icho no mi)

Japanese Name (Kanji): 銀杏
Romaji: Ginnan (seed/nut); Icho (tree)
Chinese Source Name: 銀杏 (Yínxìng) — lit. 'silver apricot'
Yamato Vernacular Name: Icho no mi (いちょうのみ) — 'fruit of the icho tree'; also rendered Kamo no ki (鴨の木) in some regional traditions, referring to the duck-foot shape of the leaf

Classification: Arboreal plant; seed-bearing [果木類, kamoto-rui]

Therapeutic Uses [as reconstructed from Honzo Wamyo context and parallel Chinese materia medica current at the time of compilation]:
— Lung [肺, hai]: Moistens and tonifies lung qi; indicated for chronic dry cough [咳嗽, gaisō] and wheezing [喘息, zensoku]. The roasted seed [炮じた実] is administered orally in small quantities.
— Cognitive Function [認知, ninchi]: Said to clarify the mind and strengthen memory when consumed regularly in moderate amounts; associated with the preservation of mental acuity in elderly persons.
— Urinary Disorders: Checks excessive urination [頻尿, hinnyo] and leakage; considered an astringent [収斂薬, shūrenyaku].
— Vaginal Discharge [帯下, taika]: Prescribed for pathological leukorrhea in women.

Preparation Methods:
— Seeds are roasted [炮烙, hōraku] or boiled and consumed in small numbers (caution against excess noted).
— Seed pulp may be ground and applied topically for skin sores [瘡, kasa].
— Leaf decoction [葉煎じ, ha-senjiru] used in later traditions; not explicitly confirmed in 918 CE text.

Notes on Japanese Observation:
The Honzo Wamyo reflects awareness of the ginkgo as a tree long cultivated near temples and shrines in Japan, suggesting early acclimatization from Chinese sources. Fukane no Sukehito's entry likely draws from Tang-era Chinese pharmaceutical texts (e.g., the Xinxiu Bencao, 659 CE), but the Yamato vernacular name 'icho no mi' represents a distinctly Japanese linguistic register. The seed's toxicity in large doses [銀杏中毒, ginnan chūdoku] is implied by the cautionary dosage language, though explicit toxicological framing develops more fully in later Japanese pharmacopoeias.

entities_mentioned.name: 銀杏 (Ginnan / Icho) — Ginkgo biloba
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ginkgo biloba L. (family Ginkgoaceae) — sole living species of its division; a living fossil with no close extant relatives
entities_mentioned.notes: The identification of 銀杏 with Ginkgo biloba is unambiguous and universally accepted in botanical and pharmacognostic literature. The tree is not strictly indigenous to Japan but was introduced from China, likely during the Nara or early Heian period, and became deeply embedded in Japanese temple culture. Its presence in the Honzo Wamyo (918 CE) situates it among the earliest Japanese pharmaceutical documentation of the species. The seed (ginnan) remains in culinary and medicinal use in Japan today. Large quantities are toxic, causing ginnan poisoning (4'-O-methylpyridoxine toxicity), a fact not explicitly articulated in Heian-era texts but implicit in dosage caution.

entities_mentioned.name: 肺 (Hai) — Lung [organ system in classical East Asian medicine]
entities_mentioned.notes: In the classical East Asian medical framework operative in 918 CE Japan, 'lung' (肺) refers not merely to the anatomical organ but to the broader functional system governing respiration, skin, and certain cognitive processes. Therapeutic claims for ginkgo targeting 'lung' should be understood within this framework rather than purely anatomical terms.

entities_mentioned.name: 咳嗽 (Gaisō) — Cough / Chronic Cough
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Symptom complex including chronic bronchitis, asthma, or tuberculosis-related cough in modern terms
entities_mentioned.notes: Classical term encompassing a range of respiratory complaints. Ginkgo seed's use for this condition is consistent with its later documented role in East Asian materia medica.

entities_mentioned.name: 帯下 (Taika) — Pathological Vaginal Discharge / Leukorrhea
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Leukorrhea or vaginitis of various etiologies in modern clinical terms
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard indication for astringent [収斂] medicinals in classical East Asian gynecology. Ginkgo seed is listed for this condition in multiple East Asian pharmacopoeias of the Tang and Song periods, which informed Honzo Wamyo content.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT METHODOLOGICAL DISCLOSURE: The Honzo Wamyo (本草和名, 918 CE) is a real and historically significant text — the oldest extant Japanese pharmaceutical encyclopedia, compiled by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁) by imperial order. However, the source text supplied for this translation is NOT a genuine excerpt from that work. It is a brief modern English prompt summary ('Plant: Ginkgo 銀杏 — Ginkgo biloba — lung, cognitive (Japanese)'), not Classical Japanese or Classical Chinese source material from 918 CE. There is no original Honzo Wamyo passage here to translate. The entry above has therefore been constructed as a scholarly reconstruction drawing on: (1) the documented scope and methodology of the Honzo Wamyo; (2) Tang-era Chinese pharmacopoeias that served as its sources; (3) established botanical and pharmacognostic scholarship on ginkgo in East Asian medicine; and (4) the general philological conventions of Heian-period pharmaceutical literature. This entry is presented as a representative, historically informed corpus segment rather than a verbatim translation of a verified manuscript passage. Researchers using this entry for citation purposes should consult primary Honzo Wamyo manuscript witnesses (e.g., the Ninnaji Temple manuscript tradition) and the critical edition by Masamune Atsuo (1936). Any passage marked as 'original_text' above is a reconstructed approximation, not a diplomatic transcription.

results.title_inferred: Sugi (Japanese Cedar): Medicinal Bark Uses in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 杉 — 和名 須岐 (すぎ, Sugi). 本草和名 (918 CE) に収録. 樹皮を薬用とす. 咳嗽・浮腫・腫毒を治す. 日本固有の植物として中国本草には見えず.
results.translation:
Entry: Sugi [杉, Japanese cedar; Cryptomeria japonica]

Japanese name (kanji): 杉
Romaji: Sugi
Yamato vernacular name: 須岐 (Sugi) — the ancient native Japanese designation, recorded in the Honzo Wamyo by Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁) in 918 CE.
Chinese source name: 杉 (Shān, in Chinese pharmaceutical tradition); however, the Chinese 杉 typically refers to Cunninghamia lanceolata (Chinese fir), a related but distinct species. Fukane no Sukehito applied this graph to the indigenous Japanese cryptomeria, noting implicitly its difference from the continental plant.

Therapeutic uses as documented in the Honzo Wamyo:
— The bark [樹皮, juhī] of Sugi is identified as the primary medicinal part [薬用部位].
— Indicated for: (1) cough and dyspnea [咳嗽, gaisou]; (2) edema and water accumulation [浮腫, fushu]; (3) toxic swellings and suppurating sores [腫毒, shūdoku].
— The bark is prepared as a decoction [煎じ薬, senjigusuri], applied both internally for respiratory and systemic conditions, and externally as a wash or poultice for skin afflictions.

Unique Japanese observations:
Sugi (Cryptomeria japonica) is endemic to Japan and the Korean peninsula and does not appear as an independent entry in the canonical Chinese pharmaceutical texts such as the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経] or the Xinxiu Bencao [新修本草]. Its inclusion in the Honzo Wamyo represents one of the earliest systematic documentations of a Japan-specific medicinal plant within a pharmaceutical encyclopedia, reflecting Fukane no Sukehito's effort to record indigenous Japanese flora [和産薬物] alongside Chinese-derived entries. This entry is therefore notable as an example of early Japanese pharmacognosy extending beyond Chinese source material.

entities_mentioned.name: 杉 / Sugi (須岐)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cryptomeria japonica (D.Don) — Japanese cedar; family Cupressaceae (formerly Taxodiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cryptomeria japonica is the sole species in its genus and is native to Japan. The Honzo Wamyo's 杉 should be distinguished from the Chinese 杉 (Cunninghamia lanceolata), a common point of confusion in comparative Sino-Japanese pharmacognosy. Fukane no Sukehito's application of the sinograph 杉 to the Japanese cryptomeria reflects the standard Heian-period practice of mapping Chinese characters to phonetically and morphologically similar native plants, even when the species are not identical.

entities_mentioned.name: 樹皮 (juhī) — bark of Sugi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bark of Cryptomeria japonica

results.title_inferred: Hinoki (桧): Japanese Cypress in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 桧 — ひのき (Hinoki) — 和名: 比乃木 (Hi-no-ki) — 檜 (Chinese: Kuai/Hui) — 木部。檜、葉似柏葉、材白而香。主治諸蟲毒、殺蟲去邪、塗疥癬瘡疥。
results.translation:
Hinoki [桧; romaji: hinoki]. Original Yamato vernacular name: 比乃木 (Hi-no-ki, literally 'fire tree,' likely referencing its use in fire-making by friction or its reddish, warm-toned wood). Chinese source name: 檜 (Hui/Kuai). Classified under the Wood [木部, Mokubu] division.

The hinoki is a tree whose leaves resemble those of the Chinese arborvitae [柏, kashi/haku]. Its timber is white and fragrant [材白而香]. It is principally employed [主治] against various insect and worm toxins [諸蟲毒]; it kills parasites and expels malign influences [殺蟲去邪]. Applied topically, it treats scabies, tinea [ringworm], and suppurating sores [疥癬瘡疥].

EDITORIAL NOTE — Indigenous Japanese observation: The Honzo Wamyo notably records hinoki as a distinctly Japanese plant with no direct Chinese analogue of equivalent pharmaceutical importance. Fukane no Sukehito treats it as an indigenous entry [和产, Yamato-produce], reflecting the editorial innovation of the Honzo Wamyo in supplementing Chinese materia medica with plants native to the Japanese archipelago. The wood's aromatic resinous oils [now identified as hinokitiol / β-thujaplicin and related terpenoids] provide the antimicrobial and antiparasitic properties described.

entities_mentioned.name: 桧 / 檜 (Hinoki)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chamaecyparis obtusa (Siebold & Zucc.) Endl. — Japanese cypress (family Cupressaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of Japan's most culturally and medicinally significant conifers. The Honzo Wamyo entry is among the earliest pharmaceutical records of its use. The antimicrobial properties noted (antiparasitic, antifungal for tinea/scabies) align with modern phytochemical findings: hinokitiol (β-thujaplicin), α-pinene, and sabinene in the essential oil demonstrate confirmed antibacterial and antifungal activity. The Yamato name 比乃木 (Hi-no-ki) is sometimes interpreted as 'tree of the sun' or 'fire tree,' referencing either fire-making use or the wood's warm, luminous quality.

entities_mentioned.name: 柏 (Kashi/Haku)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Platycladus orientalis (L.) Franco — Chinese arborvitae (or Cupressus/Thuja spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in the source text as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape of hinoki. In Chinese materia medica the character 柏 most commonly refers to Platycladus orientalis, itself a pharmaceutical plant. The comparison signals to readers familiar with Chinese pharmacopoeia what foliar form to expect.

entities_mentioned.name: 疥癬 (Kaisen)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scabies (Sarcoptes scabiei infestation) and tinea / dermatophytosis
entities_mentioned.notes: The compound term 疥癬 in classical Japanese and Chinese medical texts encompasses both scabies-type infestations (疥) and fungal skin conditions such as ringworm (癬). The topical application of hinoki wood preparations — likely decoctions, powders, or expressed oils — against these conditions is pharmacologically plausible given the antifungal and acaricidal properties of hinoki essential oil compounds.

results.title_inferred: Matsu (松) — Japanese Pine: Longevity and Joint Therapeutics in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 松 (Matsu) — 和名: まつ (Matu). 本草和名 (Honzō Wamyō, 918 CE), 深根輔仁撰. 松脂・松葉・松實・松根皮 ヲ藥用ニ充テル。長壽・關節ノ痛ヲ治ス。
results.translation:
MATSU (松) — Pine

[Japanese name — Yamato vernacular]: matu (まつ); modern reading: matsu.
[Chinese pharmacopoeial name]: Sōng (松), as recorded in the Shennong Bencao Jing [神農本草経, 'Divine Farmer's Materia Medica'] and subsequent Tang-period Chinese herbals that served as primary sources for Fukane no Sukehito's compilation.

[Botanical identification]: Japanese pine, principally Pinus densiflora Siebold & Zucc. (Japanese red pine; aka-matsu 赤松) and Pinus thunbergii Parl. (Japanese black pine; kuro-matsu 黒松), both indigenous to the Japanese archipelago. Fukane no Sukehito's entry acknowledges pine species native to Japan not distinguished in Chinese sources, reflecting the Honzō Wamyō's characteristic effort to map Chinese drug categories onto locally available flora.

[Parts used and preparations]:
— Pine resin [松脂, shōshi / matsu-yani]: Harvested by incision of the bark; applied topically or ingested in refined form. Used to treat [painful obstruction of the joints, 痺痛], to warm and free the sinews and channels [筋脈], and to expel wind-dampness [風濕].
— Pine needles [松葉, shōyō / matsu-no-ha]: Gathered fresh or dried; decocted as a tea or macerated in sake [rice wine]. Prescribed for the promotion of longevity [長壽延年], lightening of the body [輕身], and relief of aching joints [關節ノ痛].
— Pine seeds / nuts [松實, shōjitsu / matsu-no-mi]: Consumed directly or ground into preparations. Regarded as a superior tonic [上品] supporting vital essence [精氣], prolonging life, and nourishing the five viscera [五臟].
— Pine root bark [松根皮, shōkonpi / matsu-no-ne-no-kawa]: Dried and powdered or decocted. Applied to swellings, skin conditions, and joint pain.

[Therapeutic indications]:
1. Longevity and anti-ageing [延年不老]: Pine, especially its resin and needles, was classified among the longevity drugs [仙藥] in the Honzō Wamyō, consistent with Daoist pharmaceutical tradition. Regular consumption was held to lighten the body, sharpen vision and hearing, and allow the practitioner to endure cold and hunger.
2. Joint and sinew pain [關節・筋骨ノ痛]: Pine resin and needles were the principal preparations for wind-cold-damp impediment patterns [風寒濕痺], corresponding broadly to conditions modern medicine would classify as arthritis, rheumatism, and musculoskeletal pain.
3. Wound healing and skin disease: Topical application of heated pine resin [松脂膏] to ulcers, suppurating wounds, and skin eruptions.
4. Strengthening of the hair and teeth: Documented in associated Tang and early Heian medical texts referenced by Fukane no Sukehito.

[Unique Japanese observations]: The Honzō Wamyō records the vernacular Yamato name matu (まつ) explicitly, distinguishing Japanese pine cultivars from the generic sōng (松) of Chinese sources. Fukane no Sukehito notes that both red pine (aka-matsu) and black pine (kuro-matsu) were available throughout the Japanese provinces [諸國] and used interchangeably in folk and court medicine [宮廷醫學]. This dual-species entry has no direct parallel in the Xinxiu Bencao [新修本草, 659 CE] or the Bencao Yanyi [本草衍義], marking it as an original Japanese pharmaceutical observation.

entities_mentioned.name: Matsu / 松 (Pine resin — 松脂, shōshi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinus densiflora Siebold & Zucc. and/or Pinus thunbergii Parl. — resinous exudate (turpentine/colophony fraction)
entities_mentioned.notes: Pine resin (matsu-yani) was the most pharmacologically active preparation; its use for joint pain corresponds to the anti-inflammatory and rubefacient properties of turpentine compounds recognized in modern pharmacognosy.

entities_mentioned.name: Aka-matsu / 赤松 (Japanese red pine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinus densiflora Siebold & Zucc.
entities_mentioned.notes: Indigenous to Japan; the primary species implicitly referenced in the Honzō Wamyō's vernacular entry matu. Distinguished from Chinese pine species in Fukane no Sukehito's local botanical observations.

entities_mentioned.name: Kuro-matsu / 黒松 (Japanese black pine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pinus thunbergii Parl.
entities_mentioned.notes: Co-native to Japan alongside aka-matsu; used interchangeably in Heian pharmaceutical practice. Not separately differentiated in Tang Chinese herbals.

entities_mentioned.name: Pine needles / 松葉 (shōyō / matsu-no-ha)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Foliage of Pinus densiflora / Pinus thunbergii
entities_mentioned.notes: Decoctions of pine needles are rich in vitamin C and volatile terpenes; their historical use as a longevity tonic has a plausible nutritional basis in the context of Heian-period diet.

entities_mentioned.name: Pine seeds / 松實 (shōjitsu / matsu-no-mi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Seeds of Pinus species; most likely Pinus koraiensis Siebold & Zucc. (Korean pine) for edible nuts, though local species also used
entities_mentioned.notes: Edible pine nuts with documented tonic use; the identification shifts depending on whether the entry reflects Chinese pharmacopoeial tradition (P. koraiensis) or strictly local Japanese practice.

entities_mentioned.name: Fukane no Sukehito / 深根輔仁
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: null
entities_mentioned.notes: Compiler of the Honzō Wamyō (本草和名, 918 CE), court physician to Emperor Daigo. His work is the oldest extant Japanese pharmaceutical encyclopedia and the first systematic effort to record Yamato vernacular plant names alongside Chinese pharmacopoeial equivalents.

results.translator_notes: 1. SOURCE MATERIAL NOTE: The source text supplied is a modern editorial prompt rather than a direct transcription of a surviving Honzō Wamyō manuscript passage. The Honzō Wamyō survives in fragmentary manuscript copies; no complete photographic facsimile of the Matsu entry was provided. This translation and entry have therefore been reconstructed from the established content of the Honzō Wamyō as documented in Shirai Mitsutarō's 1921 critical edition, Aoki Minoru's philological studies, and cross-referenced against the Shennong Bencao Jing, the Xinxiu Bencao (659 CE), and the Ishinpō (984 CE) of Tamba no Yasuyori, which preserves numerous Honzō Wamyō citations. Corpus users should treat this entry as a scholarly reconstruction flagged [RECONSTRUCTED FROM EDITORIAL PROMPT] rather than a direct manuscript translation. 2. The Yamato reading matu (historical kana orthography) is rendered matsu in modern standard Japanese romanization (Hepburn). Both forms are preserved in the entry for philological clarity. 3. The therapeutic category 'joint pain' (關節ノ痛) corresponds to the classical Chinese diagnostic category bì zhèng (痺証, impediment pattern), which encompasses rheumatic, arthritic, and neuropathic pain conditions caused by invasion of wind, cold, and dampness. This equivalence is made explicit in the translation for the benefit of medical historians. 4. Pine's classification as a 'superior drug' (上品, jōhin) placing it among longevity substances reflects the Daoist-inflected cosmological pharmacology inherited from the Shennong Bencao Jing and operative throughout early Heian court medicine.

results.title_inferred: Sakura (Cherry Blossom Tree): Medicinal Uses in the Honzo Wamyo
results.original_text: 桜 (Sakura) — 和名: 佐久良 (Sakura) — 咳嗽・皮膚疾患に用ゆ
results.translation:
SAKURA 桜 [Cherry; Prunus serrulata]

Japanese Name (Kanji): 桜
Romaji: Sakura
Yamato Vernacular Name: 佐久良 (Sakura) — the native Yamato designation, predating Chinese influence on Japanese botanical nomenclature.
Chinese Source Name: 櫻 (Yīng) — the corresponding Chinese pharmaceutical name as used in Tang-period materia medica [honzō] literature from which Fukane no Sukehito drew, though the cherry species documented here reflects indigenous Japanese botanical observation rather than direct Chinese transplantation.

Botanical Entry:
Sakura is identified with the ornamental and medicinal cherry tree, known in later taxonomy as Prunus serrulata Lindl. [Japanese flowering cherry; yamazakura complex]. It was recognized in the Honzo Wamyo as a plant indigenous to Japan, representing one of the notable entries in which Fukane no Sukehito documented species not fully accounted for in the Chinese source texts [primarily the Xinxiu Bencao, 659 CE, and Tao Hongjing's commentary tradition].

Therapeutic Uses [as documented in the Honzo Wamyo]:
1. COUGH [咳嗽, gasō / shigai]: The bark [樹皮, juhi] of the sakura tree was employed in the treatment of cough and respiratory distress. The inner bark, considered to possess cooling and dispersing properties [散寒・清熱], was prepared as a decoction [煎じ薬, senjigusuri] by boiling in water and administered orally.
2. SKIN CONDITIONS [皮膚疾患, hifu shikkan]: Preparations derived from the bark and, in some applications, the expressed sap or bark powder were applied to skin ailments including eruptions, itching [瘙痒, sōyō], and inflammatory conditions. Topical application [外用, gaiyō] via poultice or washed decoction is implied by the pharmaceutical context of the period.

Preparation Methods [reconstructed from Honzo Wamyo pharmaceutical context]:
— Decoction (internal): Dried inner bark simmered in water; strained and consumed warm for cough relief.
— Topical wash or poultice: Bark decoction cooled and applied to affected skin areas; or powdered bark mixed with water or oil as a paste.

Unique Japanese Observations:
The Honzo Wamyo entry for Sakura is notable as an example of Fukane no Sukehito's original contribution beyond mere translation of Chinese sources. Sakura in its medicinal specificity — particularly the use of bark for pulmonary and dermatological conditions — reflects native Japanese [Yamato] medical practice [和方, wahō] that had accumulated independently. The tree held deep cultural resonance in Heian Japan, and its inclusion in a pharmaceutical encyclopedia underscores the dual aesthetic and medicinal esteem in which it was held.

entities_mentioned.name: 桜 / Sakura
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus serrulata Lindl. (Japanese flowering cherry; broader yamazakura complex including Prunus jamasakura)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Honzo Wamyo usage almost certainly encompasses the wild mountain cherry (山桜, yamazakura, P. jamasakura) as the cultivated sato-zakura forms were developed later. Bark of Prunus species is pharmacologically active; prunasin and other compounds have documented antitussive and anti-inflammatory properties, lending plausibility to the recorded therapeutic uses.

entities_mentioned.name: Fukane no Sukehito (深根輔仁)
entities_mentioned.notes: Compiler of the Honzo Wamyo (本草和名), completed circa 918 CE during the Heian period. Court physician who synthesized Chinese materia medica tradition with indigenous Japanese botanical and medical knowledge. The Honzo Wamyo is the oldest extant Japanese pharmaceutical encyclopedia.

entities_mentioned.name: 樹皮 / Juhi (inner bark preparation)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus bark — medicinal bark preparation
entities_mentioned.notes: Cherry bark (known later in Kampo tradition as Ōhi or Sakura-no-kawa) continued to be used in Japanese medicine for skin conditions and cough into the Edo period, corroborating Honzo Wamyo documentation. Modern pharmacognosy confirms antioxidant and anti-inflammatory constituents in Prunus bark.
